title: American Literature
intro: American Literature questions span every form the category recognizes — lyric and narrative poetry, the novel and short story, drama, and a native tradition of essayistic nonfiction — and quizbowl organizes almost all of it into the same handful of movements that literary history uses: a colonial beginning, a nineteenth-century flowering split between prose romance and poetry, a realist-to-modernist turn, and a broad postwar field that fragments into competing schools of fiction, poetry, and drama. Emily Dickinson tops the frequency table outright, and poets crowd the upper ranks alongside novelists and playwrights to a degree unusual among national literatures, which is why poetry gets treated as its own recurring thread rather than folded into prose-era sections.

The colonial and early national period is thin but distinctive: Puritan devotional poets Anne Bradstreet and Edward Taylor, the enslaved poet Phillis Wheatley, Benjamin Franklin's autobiographical persona, and the early tales of Washington Irving ("Rip Van Winkle," "The Legend of Sleepy Hollow") and James Fenimore Cooper's Leatherstocking novels (Natty Bumppo, The Last of the Mohicans) carry the era. Transcendentalism supplies the philosophical core of the mid-century — Emerson's essays and Thoreau's Walden and "Civil Disobedience" — while the American Renaissance proper is a fiction cluster answering to Hawthorne (The Scarlet Letter, "Young Goodman Brown," "Rappaccini's Daughter") and Melville (Moby-Dick, "Bartleby, the Scrivener," Billy Budd). Nineteenth-century poetry runs in parallel: Whitman's Leaves of Grass and Dickinson's lyrics anchor it, Poe supplies both verse ("The Raven") and a separate strand of Gothic and detective short fiction, and the fireside poets — Longfellow, Whittier, Bryant — round out the schoolroom canon.

Late-century realism (Twain, Henry James, Howells), regionalism (Kate Chopin's The Awakening, Sarah Orne Jewett, Bret Harte's local color), and naturalism (Stephen Crane, Frank Norris, Theodore Dreiser, Jack London) mark literature's turn toward documentary detail and social determinism, setting up the modernist rupture. Modernist fiction is the Lost Generation's territory — Fitzgerald's Gatsby and Tender Is the Night, Hemingway's spare Paris-and-war novels, Gertrude Stein's expatriate salon, Sherwood Anderson's Winesburg, Ohio — while modernist poetry answers to a denser, more technical set of names: Eliot and Pound as the movement's theorists, Wallace Stevens and William Carlos Williams as its most-asked American-based figures, plus Marianne Moore, H.D. and Imagism, and Hart Crane's The Bridge.

Two lineages cut across these eras rather than sitting inside a single decade. Southern literature is a self-contained regional canon running from Faulkner's Yoknapatawpha novels through Flannery O'Connor's grotesque short stories, Robert Penn Warren, Eudora Welty, Carson McCullers, and — as a later inheritor of the mode — Cormac McCarthy. African American literature forms its own continuous arc: Wheatley and Frederick Douglass's slave narrative at one end, Du Bois and Dunbar bridging to the Harlem Renaissance of Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, and Jean Toomer, and then a postwar continuation — Ellison's Invisible Man, Baldwin, Richard Wright, Toni Morrison, Alice Walker, Gwendolyn Brooks, Maya Angelou — that is asked about as heavily as any other twentieth-century cluster.

American drama is unusually prominent for a national literature, holding its own slot across O'Neill, Tennessee Williams, Arthur Miller, Albee, Lorraine Hansberry, and August Wilson, plus individually-answered plays. Postwar poetry splits into the confessional line (Lowell, Plath, Sexton, Berryman) and the Beats (Ginsberg's "Howl," Kerouac's On the Road), while postwar fiction (1945–1980) runs from Salinger and Bellow through Vonnegut, Pynchon, Updike, and Roth, and contemporary fiction since 1980 continues it through DeLillo, David Foster Wallace, Jhumpa Lahiri, and Colson Whitehead. A genre-fiction strand — Poe's detective tales at its root, then Bradbury, Le Guin, and Lovecraft in science fiction and horror, Chandler in hardboiled crime — sits alongside the literary mainstream. Finally, a large body of common-link material recurs across all of these writers: places (New York City, California, Mississippi, Harlem), historical backdrops (the Civil War, the World Wars, Vietnam), bare first names shared by multiple characters, and the occasional non-American author (Tolstoy, Kafka, Borges) who surfaces because an American work engages with them directly.

sections.name: Colonial and Early National Literature
sections.blurb: Colonial Puritan verse and early national prose forge an American voice, from Bradstreet's devotional poetry through Irving's and Cooper's frontier tales.

entries.note: Puritan poet, the first published poet of the American colonies; questions cite The Tenth Muse Lately Sprung Up in America, "To My Dear and Loving Husband," and "Upon the Burning of Our House."

works.note: Her husband, addressed in "To My Dear and Loving Husband" and "A Letter to Her Husband, Absent upon Publick Employment"; subject of a John Berryman homage.

entries.note: Puritan minister-poet unpublished until the 20th century; "Huswifery" ("Make me, O Lord, thy spinning wheel complete") and his Preparatory Meditations exemplify his knotty "American Baroque" style.
"Poet of the American Revolution"; wrote "The Wild Honey Suckle," the captivity account The British Prison-Ship, and the graveyard meditation "The Indian Burying Ground."

works.note: Poem on a Native burial site where the dead sit upright; "Reason's self shall bow the knee / To shadows and delusions" recurs in clues.

entries.note: Early national poet-diplomat; wrote the mock-epic "The Hasty Pudding" praising corn and the more ambitious epic The Columbiad on Christopher Columbus.
Revolutionary-era playwright and historian; satirized Massachusetts governor Thomas Hutchinson as "Rapatio" in The Adulateur, and wrote an early History of the American Revolution.
Wrote under pseudonyms like "Silence Dogood" and "Poor Richard"; Poor Richard's Almanack and his posthumously published Autobiography are his most-clued works.
Early American Gothic novelist; in Wieland the biloquist Carwin dupes siblings Clara and Theodore, while Edgar Huntly and the yellow-fever novel Arthur Mervyn are also clued.
Common-link genre spanning Brockden Brown's Wieland, its European models Walpole and Radcliffe, and later American horror writers like Poe.
Early national sketch writer behind "Rip Van Winkle" and "The Legend of Sleepy Hollow" from The Sketch Book; also wrote A History of New York as Diedrich Knickerbocker.

works.note: Henpecked villager who drinks with Hendrick Hudson's ghostly crew and sleeps twenty years, waking after the American Revolution.
Miser Tom Walker trades his soul to "Old Scratch" for Captain Kidd's buried treasure and becomes a ruthless moneylender.

entries.note: Author of the Leatherstocking Tales starring Natty Bumppo — The Last of the Mohicans, The Deerslayer, The Pioneers; Twain's essay on his "literary offenses" is a stock clue.

works.note: Second Leatherstocking Tale; Magua's massacre outside Fort William Henry and the clifftop deaths of Uncas and Cora close the novel.
Cooper's frontiersman hero, called Hawkeye, Deerslayer, or the trapper across the five Leatherstocking Tales; travels with Mohican companion Chingachgook.
Earliest-set Leatherstocking Tale; young Bumppo earns his nickname at Glimmerglass Lake alongside Hurry Harry and the Hutter sisters, Hetty and Judith.

entries.note: First bestselling American novelist; her sentimental Charlotte Temple, about a schoolgirl seduced by John Montraville, was often called the country's first novel.

works.note: Title schoolgirl seduced by soldier John Montraville and abandoned in New York, dying after giving birth to her daughter Lucy.

sections.name: Transcendentalism and the American Renaissance
sections.blurb: Poe, Hawthorne, and Melville plumb sin and obsession in the Dark Romantic mode, while Emerson, Thoreau, and Margaret Fuller's Transcendentalist circle prizes self-reliant intuition.

entries.note: Idealist literary movement centered on Emerson, Thoreau, and Fuller's Dial circle and the utopian Brook Farm community; stressed intuition and the individual soul over doctrine.
Central Transcendentalist essayist; "Self-Reliance," Nature with its "transparent eyeball," "The American Scholar," and the Divinity School Address dominate clues.

works.note: Emerson's essay urging nonconformity and self-trust ("Trust thyself: every heart vibrates to that iron string"); with Nature, his best-known work.

entries.note: Transcendentalist who lived at Walden Pond; Walden and "Civil Disobedience" provide nearly all clues, often alongside his night in jail and Emerson's patronage.

works.note: Thoreau's memoir of his two years, two months, and two days at Walden Pond, including the chapters "Economy" and "Brute Neighbors"; champions living "deliberately."
Concord pond, owned by Emerson, where Thoreau built his cabin and watched the "Battle of the Ants."
Essay defending refusal to pay taxes that support slavery and the Mexican War, inspired by Thoreau's night in jail.

entries.note: Transcendentalist first editor of The Dial; wrote the feminist Woman in the Nineteenth Century (expanded from "The Great Lawsuit") and the travelogue Summer on the Lakes.
Author of Little Women and its sequels; questions cite the four March sisters, Hospital Sketches, and the sensation fiction she published as A. M. Barnard.

works.note: Novel of the four March sisters' Civil War-era coming of age; Jo rejects Laurie's proposal and marries Professor Bhaer instead.
Tomboyish second March sister who sells her hair for money, burns Amy's manuscript, and later runs the Plumfield school for boys.
Common-link family of sisters Meg, Jo, Beth, and Amy in Little Women; Beth dies of scarlet fever after delivering charity to the Hummels.

entries.note: Master of Gothic tales and musical verse: "The Raven," "The Tell-Tale Heart," "The Fall of the House of Usher," and "The Cask of Amontillado," plus detective Auguste Dupin.

works.note: Opium-addled narrator's dead first wife Ligeia returns from beyond, reincarnated in his second wife Rowena's body; recites the poem "The Conqueror Worm."
Poem embedded in "Ligeia" describing angels who watch a "blood-red thing" devour actors in a theater; ends, "the hero [is] the Conqueror Worm."
Ancestral mansion of twins Roderick and Madeline in "The Fall of the House of Usher"; collapses into a tarn after Madeline's premature burial.
Montresor lures the boastful Fortunato into the catacombs with promised wine and walls him up alive in revenge.
Inquisition prisoner narrowly escapes a swinging blade and closing walls before General Lasalle's army frees him from Toledo.
Fisherman narrator's hair turns white surviving a Norwegian whirlpool by lashing himself to a cask, unlike his drowned brother.
Essay-turned-story on the perverse urge to self-destruct; its narrator murders with a poisoned candle, then confesses despite getting away with it.
Onomatopoeic poem coining "tintinnabulation," cataloguing silver, golden, brazen, and iron bells across four stanzas of mounting terror.
Grieving narrator questions a talking raven perched on a bust of Pallas about his lost love Lenore; it answers only "Nevermore."
Poe's "ratiocination"-practicing detective of "The Murders in the Rue Morgue," "The Purloined Letter," and "The Mystery of Marie Rogêt."

works.topic: The Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym of Nantucket
works.answerline: The Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym of Nantucket
works.note: Poe's sole completed novel, ending abruptly as a shrouded white giant appears near the South Pole; Toni Morrison's Playing in the Dark reads its racial imagery.
Poe's only novel's title character, a Nantucket stowaway; survives mutiny and cannibalism before the eerie white figure appears near the South Pole.

entries.note: Dark romancer of Puritan New England: The Scarlet Letter, The House of the Seven Gables, and tales like "Young Goodman Brown," "The Minister's Black Veil," and "Rappaccini's Daughter."

works.note: Hawthorne's novel of Hester Prynne, Pearl, Dimmesdale, and Chillingworth in Puritan Boston; the three scaffold scenes and "The Custom-House" introduction recur in clues.
Adulteress who embroiders the scarlet "A," raises daughter Pearl, and shields former lover Dimmesdale until his scaffold confession and death.
Autobiographical preface to The Scarlet Letter; narrator finds Surveyor Jonathan Pue's manuscript and a scarlet "A" while working at the Salem customhouse.

works.topic: The House of the Seven Gables
works.answerline: The House of the Seven Gables
works.note: Novel of the cursed Pyncheon family mansion, the aged Hepzibah, and her cousin Judge Pyncheon's mysterious death in his ancestral chair.
Padua tale of Beatrice, poisoned by her father Dr. Rappaccini's toxic garden, who dies drinking a rival scientist's antidote.
Puritan Goodman Brown leaves wife Faith for a satanic forest ritual, leaving him unable to trust his neighbors' piety afterward.
Reverend Hooper dons an unexplained black veil for life, unsettling his congregation and fiancée Elizabeth until his deathbed.
Scientist Aylmer, aided by servant Aminadab, kills his wife Georgiana trying to remove her "Crimson Hand" birthmark with an elixir.
Puritan leader John Endicott cuts down the title Maypole and breaks up the wedding revels of Edgar and Edith.
Doctor gives elderly friends Fountain-of-Youth water, reviving a withered rose and their youth before both fade again; owns a "Forbear!"-uttering Hippocrates bust.

entries.note: Author of Moby-Dick, Billy Budd, "Bartleby, the Scrivener," and "Benito Cereno"; clues range from the early Polynesian novels Typee and Omoo to The Confidence-Man.

works.note: Melville's whaling epic narrated by Ishmael; Ahab, Queequeg, Starbuck, the Pequod, and chapters like "The Whiteness of the Whale" are stock clues.
Narrator of Moby-Dick ("Call me Ishmael"), a damp-November misanthrope who befriends Queequeg and survives the Pequod's sinking by clinging to a coffin.
Monomaniacal, peg-legged Pequod captain hunting Moby Dick for biting off his leg; dies harpooning the whale, strangled by his own line.
Tattooed Pacific islander harpooner and prince of Kokovoko who worships the idol Yojo; his coffin later saves Ishmael's life.
Novella in which Amasa Delano boards the San Dominick without realizing captain Cereno has been deposed by Babo's slave revolt.
Handsome sailor impressed onto the Bellipotent who fatally strikes master-at-arms John Claggart after a false mutiny accusation, then hangs.
Bellipotent master-at-arms who falsely accuses Billy Budd of mutiny and is killed by Budd's punch; Melville's narrator cites his "natural depravity."

works.topic: "Bartleby, the Scrivener: A Story of Wall Street"
works.answerline: "Bartleby, the Scrivener: A Story of Wall Street"
works.note: Wall Street copyist who "would prefer not to" work, and eventually starves in the Tombs; the narrator recalls his rumored Dead Letter Office past.
Melville story collection including "Benito Cereno," "Bartleby, the Scrivener," "The Bell-Tower," and the Galapagos sketches "The Encantadas."

sections.name: Nineteenth-Century Poetry
sections.blurb: American poetry's founding century: the moralizing popular verse of Bryant and the Fireside Poets gives way to Whitman's sprawling free verse and Dickinson's compressed, dash-driven lyrics.

entries.note: Early Romantic poet and longtime New York Evening Post editor; "Thanatopsis," drafted in his teens, and "To a Waterfowl" are his near-universal clues.

works.note: Meditation on death urging readers to join the "innumerable caravan" and lie down "to pleasant dreams"; Greek title means a view of death.

entries.note: The most famous Fireside Poet: The Song of Hiawatha, Evangeline, "Paul Revere's Ride," and "A Psalm of Life" supply most clues.

works.note: Ojibwe legendary hero of Longfellow's epic, raised by Nokomis on Lake Superior's shore; woos Minnehaha in trochaic tetrameter verse.
Longfellow's epic in unrhymed trochaic tetrameter modeled on the Finnish Kalevala; follows the Ojibwe hero Hiawatha through legend and courtship.
Longfellow's exhortation that "Life is real! Life is earnest!," urging readers to leave "footprints on the sands of time."
Longfellow poem of a smith who "looks the whole world in the face, for he owes not any man," working his forge beneath a chestnut tree.
Longfellow's narrative poem of Acadian lovers separated by the British expulsion; Evangeline searches for Gabriel for decades.
Longfellow's frame narrative of travelers swapping stories, including the collection's most famous tale, "Paul Revere's Ride."

entries.note: Quaker abolitionist Fireside Poet; the winter idyll Snow-Bound anchors nearly every question, with "Barbara Frietchie" and "Maud Muller" as supporting clues.
Fireside poet and first editor of the Atlantic Monthly; A Fable for Critics satirizes his contemporaries, and The Biglow Papers use Yankee dialect.
Fireside poet and Boston physician who coined "anesthesia" and "Boston Brahmin"; "Old Ironsides," "The Chambered Nautilus," The Autocrat of the Breakfast-Table.

works.note: Meditation on a "ship of pearl" mollusk shell, urging the soul to "build thee more stately mansions" as the creature's spiral grows.

entries.note: Free-verse poet of Leaves of Grass; "Song of Myself," the Lincoln elegies "O Captain! My Captain!" and "When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd," and "Crossing Brooklyn Ferry" recur.

works.note: Whitman's lifelong, endlessly revised collection, first published 1855 with a preface calling America "essentially the greatest poem"; contains "Song of Myself."
Fifty-two-section centerpiece of Leaves of Grass; "I celebrate myself, and sing myself," the child asking "What is the grass?," and the "barbaric yawp."
Catalogues the human body, including a slave-auction scene; Ray Bradbury reused the title for a story and collection.

works.topic: "When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd"
works.answerline: "When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd"
works.note: Lincoln elegy in which a hermit thrush sings "Come lovely and soothing death" as the speaker mourns "with ever-returning spring."
Speaker addresses future ferry riders, "Flood-tide below me!," insisting "distance avails not" across "scores or hundreds of years."
Boy on Paumanok's shore hears a mockingbird mourn its lost mate, learning the sea's "low and delicious word death."
Whitman's central image, "the beautiful uncut hair of graves" in "Song of Myself"; also titles a Carl Sandburg poem set at Austerlitz and Waterloo.
Catalogues workers' "varied carols" — mechanics, carpenters, boatmen, shoemakers — each singing what belongs to them alone.
Speaker flees a lecture's "proofs, the figures" for the "mystical moist night-air" to look "in perfect silence at the stars."
Purple flowers of the Lincoln elegy's title, a sprig broken for the passing coffin; also title Amy Lowell's New England poem.
Whitman's Algonquian name for his birthplace, Long Island; setting of "Out of the Cradle Endlessly Rocking" and "Starting from Paumanok."

entries.note: Common-link answerline: Whitman "loafes and invites" it in "Song of Myself," and Dickinson's poems personify it "shutting the Door" and selecting "her own Society."

The most-asked answer in American literature: the reclusive Amherst poet of "Because I could not stop for Death," "I heard a Fly buzz," and "Hope is the thing with feathers," identified by dashes and slant rhyme.

works.topic: "Because I could not stop for Death"
works.answerline: "Because I could not stop for Death"
works.note: Death personified as a courteous carriage driver who takes the speaker past a school, fields of grain, and the setting sun toward eternity.
Mysterious addressee of three passionate, unsent Dickinson letters signed "Daisy"; scholars have proposed Charles Wadsworth, Samuel Bowles, Otis Lord, or God.
Speaker as an "Inebriate of air" and "Debauchee of Dew," drunk on nature; ends with "Seraphs swing their snowy Hats" at her leaning against the sun.
Common-link concept in several Dickinson poems: "a bee" with "a song," "a sting," "a wing"; also called "a fickle food" that "does not stay."

entries.note: Jewish American poet of "The New Colossus," inscribed on the Statue of Liberty's pedestal ("give me your tired, your poor"); also wrote Songs of a Semite.
"Hoosier poet" from Indiana known for dialect verse; "Little Orphant Annie" and "The Raggedy Man," and championed fellow dialect poet Paul Laurence Dunbar.

sections.name: Realism, Regionalism, and Naturalism
sections.blurb: The post-Civil War turn from Romantic idealism to hard-eyed observation: Howells and James's psychological realism, regional local color, and naturalism's deterministic pessimism through World War I.

entries.note: Post-Civil War movement toward objective, everyday subject matter and plain style, reacting against Romantic idealization; Howells and James are its leading theorists.
The "Dean of American Letters," realist novelist and Atlantic Monthly editor; The Rise of Silas Lapham and A Hazard of New Fortunes are the core clues.

works.note: Self-made paint magnate whose social climbing and a shady land deal test his morals in Howells's The Rise of Silas Lapham.
Basil March starts a New York magazine amid the class tensions of an 1890s streetcar strike that turns violent.

entries.note: Expatriate master of the psychological novel: The Portrait of a Lady, The Turn of the Screw, The Ambassadors, Daisy Miller, and The Wings of the Dove.

works.note: Novella of a brash, unchaperoned American girl in Europe whose flirtations scandalize expatriate society; she dies of "Roman fever."
Lambert Strether travels to Paris to retrieve Chad Newsome for his fiancée, but falls under the city's spell himself.
Ghost story of a governess convinced her charges Miles and Flora are possessed by the dead servants Quint and Jessel.
Novella of John Marcher, who waits his life for a catastrophic fate and realizes too late it was letting May Bartram's love pass him by.
Feminist reformer Olive Chancellor and her cousin Basil Ransom vie for the affections of the young orator Verena Tarrant.
Isabel Archer inherits a fortune and is manipulated by Madame Merle into marrying the cold aesthete Gilbert Osmond.
Plain, wealthy Catherine Sloper is courted by the fortune-hunting Morris Townsend against her father's wishes.
Dying heiress Milly Theale is courted by Merton Densher, who is secretly engaged to the scheming Kate Croy.

entries.note: Samuel Clemens, humorist of the Mississippi: Adventures of Huckleberry Finn, Tom Sawyer, "The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County," A Connecticut Yankee, and Pudd'nhead Wilson.

works.note: Enslaved man who escapes down the Mississippi with Huck Finn; Percival Everett's 2024 novel James retells the story from his viewpoint.
Twain's novel of Huck and Jim rafting down the Mississippi; the Duke and the Dauphin, the Grangerford feud, and "All right, then, I'll go to hell" recur as clues.
Huck himself, the novel's narrator, first introduced as Tom Sawyer's friend in The Adventures of Tom Sawyer.
Huck's mischievous friend who engineers Jim's overly elaborate "rescue" at novel's end; gets lost in a cave with Becky Thatcher in his own book.
Twain's semi-autobiographical account of his years prospecting and reporting across the Nevada silver-mining West.

works.topic: "The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County"
works.answerline: "The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County"
works.note: Twain's breakout tall tale of Jim Smiley's trained frog Dan'l Webster, secretly weighted down with buckshot by a rival.
A stranger's sack of "gold" coins exposes the hypocrisy of the town's nineteen supposedly incorruptible leading citizens.
The self-righteous town whose leading citizens are exposed as frauds by a stranger's revenge scheme in Twain's "The Man That Corrupted Hadleyburg."

works.topic: A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court
works.answerline: A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court
works.note: Hank Morgan is transported to Camelot, where his modern know-how and gunpowder upend Arthurian society.
Twain's satirical travelogue of an American pleasure cruise through Europe and the Holy Land.

entries.note: Abolitionist author of Uncle Tom's Cabin, the century's best-selling novel; clues add A Key to Uncle Tom's Cabin, Dred, and Oldtown Folks.

works.note: Stowe's 1852 anti-slavery novel: Tom, Little Eva, Eliza crossing the icy Ohio, Topsy, and the brutal overseer Simon Legree are the standard clues.

variants.answerline: Uncle Tom's Cabin; or, Life Among the Lowly

works.note: Enslaved man of unwavering Christian faith whose death at Simon Legree's hands made his name a byword, now pejorative, for servile deference.

entries.note: Local colorist of Gold Rush California; "The Luck of Roaring Camp" and "The Outcasts of Poker Flat" provide nearly every clue.

works.note: Gold Rush outcasts, including the gambler John Oakhurst and the prostitute Duchess, are exiled from camp and die in a mountain snowstorm.
A mining camp adopts an orphaned baby, "the Luck," whose death in a flood devastates the reformed miners.

entries.note: Maine regionalist of The Country of the Pointed Firs and "A White Heron," in which young Sylvia refuses to reveal the heron's nest to a visiting ornithologist.

works.topic: The Country of the Pointed Firs
works.answerline: The Country of the Pointed Firs
works.note: Loosely linked sketches of a summer visitor's stay in the Maine fishing village of Dunnet Landing.
Young Sylvia climbs a great pine to spot a rare white heron, then refuses to reveal its nest to a visiting ornithologist.

entries.note: Georgia journalist who recorded plantation folktales as the Uncle Remus dialect stories of the trickster Br'er Rabbit outwitting Br'er Fox.
Louisiana local colorist; The Awakening plus the stories "The Story of an Hour" and "Désirée's Baby" make her a fixture at every difficulty.

works.note: Chopin's 1899 novel of Edna Pontellier's sexual and artistic awakening at Grand Isle, ending as she swims out into the Gulf; Robert Lebrun and Mademoiselle Reisz appear in clues.
Armand rejects his wife Désirée over their baby's complexion, then discovers after her disappearance that his own mother was Black.
Louise Mallard secretly rejoices at news of her husband's death, then dies of shock when he turns out alive.
Adopted foundling wife of Armand Aubigny, cast out over her baby's race in Chopin's "Désirée's Baby."
Protagonist of The Awakening who leaves her family in pursuit of artistic and sexual independence, ending in her swim into the Gulf.

entries.note: Feminist writer of "The Yellow Wallpaper" and the all-female utopia Herland; her treatise Women and Economics also surfaces in Social Science questions.

works.note: Woman confined for a "rest cure" comes to see a creeping woman trapped behind the wallpaper; protests S. Weir Mitchell's treatment of nervous disorders.

entries.note: Pen name of William Sydney Porter, the twist-ending story writer: "The Gift of the Magi," "The Ransom of Red Chief," and "The Cop and the Anthem"; he began writing in prison.

works.note: Two kidnappers abduct a small-town boy so unruly that they end up paying his father to take him back.
Della sells her hair to buy Jim a watch chain, while he sells his watch to buy her hair combs.

entries.note: Sardonic Civil War veteran: "An Occurrence at Owl Creek Bridge," "Chickamauga," and The Devil's Dictionary; his 1913 disappearance into revolutionary Mexico is a favorite clue.

works.note: Confederate saboteur Peyton Farquhar imagines a miraculous escape while being hanged from the title bridge; the escape is his dying hallucination.
A deaf-mute boy plays soldier in the woods, then wanders a corpse-strewn battlefield to find his own home burned and mother dead.
Carlos Fuentes's novel fictionalizing Bierce's own disappearance in the Mexican Revolution, following an old American drawn into Pancho Villa's forces.

entries.note: Naturalist who died at twenty-eight: The Red Badge of Courage, Maggie: A Girl of the Streets, "The Open Boat," and the poetry collection War Is Kind.

works.note: Crane's Civil War novel of Henry Fleming, who flees his first battle and envies the wounded their "red badge"; the tall soldier Jim Conklin's death is a stock clue.
Four men, based on Crane's own shipwreck, struggle in a small dinghy through rough seas; only the oiler drowns just short of shore.

works.topic: The Black Riders and Other Lines
works.answerline: The Black Riders and Other Lines
works.note: Crane's spare, unrhymed free-verse debut poetry collection, including a poem about a man eating his own heart in the desert.
Marshal Jack Potter brings home a new bride just as gunman Scratchy Wilson comes looking for a showdown, which the marriage defuses.
Slum girl driven to prostitution and death after her seduction by Pete in Crane's Maggie: A Girl of the Streets.

works.topic: Maggie: A Girl of the Streets
works.answerline: Maggie: A Girl of the Streets
works.note: Naturalist novella of a Bowery girl's ruin after abandonment by her lover Pete; self-published by Crane under a pseudonym.

entries.note: Naturalist of McTeague and the unfinished "Epic of Wheat" trilogy (The Octopus, The Pit), depicting deterministic economic forces crushing individuals.

works.note: Dentist McTeague murders his miserly wife Trina for her lottery winnings and dies handcuffed to his rival Marcus in Death Valley.

entries.note: Klondike naturalist: The Call of the Wild's dog Buck, White Fang, "To Build a Fire," and The Sea-Wolf's captain Wolf Larsen dominate questions.

works.note: An unnamed man ignores warnings and freezes to death in the Yukon cold after failing to relight his fire, accompanied only by his wolf-dog.
Domesticated dog Buck is stolen and sold into the Klondike, gradually reverting to his wild wolf ancestry.
Humphrey van Weyden is shipwrecked aboard the seal-hunting schooner Ghost, ruled by the brutal, Nietzschean Captain Wolf Larsen.
Dystopian novel narrated through Avis Everhard's rediscovered memoir of a fascist oligarchy crushing an American socialist revolution.

entries.note: Naturalist of Sister Carrie, tracing Carrie Meeber's rise and Hurstwood's ruin, and An American Tragedy, in which Clyde Griffiths lets Roberta Alden drown.

works.note: Carrie Meeber rises from rural poverty to stage stardom in Chicago and New York while her lover Hurstwood steals money and sinks into ruin.
Clyde Griffiths lets pregnant, working-class Roberta Alden drown rather than lose his chance at a wealthy fiancée; based on a real murder case.
Poor Ohio girl bears an illegitimate daughter and endures years as a rich man's mistress in Dreiser's second novel.

entries.note: Muckraking naturalist best known for The Jungle's exposé of the meatpacking industry; also wrote the oil-industry novel Oil! and the polemic The Brass Check.

works.note: Lithuanian immigrant Jurgis Rudkus endures Chicago meatpacking-plant horrors before finding hope in socialism; helped spur the Pure Food and Drug Act.
Bunny Ross navigates his oil-baron father's business and his own radical politics in Sinclair's novel, loosely adapted into There Will Be Blood.

entries.note: Novelist of Old New York society: The House of Mirth, The Age of Innocence with Newland Archer and Ellen Olenska, and the bleak New England novella Ethan Frome.

works.note: Wharton's novel of Lily Bart's social fall in New York, ending with her chloral overdose; Lawrence Selden and the tableaux vivants scene are common clues.
Newland Archer, engaged to conventional May Welland, falls for her scandalous cousin Countess Ellen Olenska in 1870s New York.
Title farmer's affair with his wife Zeena's cousin Mattie Silver ends in a failed suicide-pact sled crash that leaves both maimed.
Scandalous, separated cousin who draws Newland Archer's forbidden love away from his fiancée May in The Age of Innocence.
Beautiful but penniless socialite whose gambling debts and dashed marriage prospects doom her to a fatal overdose in The House of Mirth.
Ruthlessly ambitious Undine Spragg claws upward through a string of marriages in Wharton's satire of American social climbing.
Two old friends reminisce on a Roman terrace, revealing a decades-old rivalry and a secret about a daughter's true parentage.

entries.note: Novelist of the Nebraska prairie and the Southwest: My Ántonia, O Pioneers!, The Song of the Lark, and Death Comes for the Archbishop.

works.note: First of Cather's prairie trilogy: Alexandra Bergson builds up the Nebraska farm, while her brother Emil and Marie Shabata are shot under a mulberry tree by Marie's husband Frank.
Thea Kronborg, a small-town Colorado girl, develops her voice and leaves for Chicago to become an opera singer.
Episodic novel of Bishop Jean Marie Latour establishing a Catholic diocese in the New Mexico desert.
Jim Burden narrates his boyhood friendship with the resilient Bohemian immigrant girl Ántonia Shimerda on the Nebraska prairie.
Godfrey St. Peter grows detached from his family, dwelling instead on student Tom Outland's discovery of Blue Mesa cliff dwellings.

entries.note: Historian and memoirist, grandson and great-grandson of presidents; The Education of Henry Adams and Mont-Saint-Michel and Chartres are his major prose works.

works.note: Adams's ironic third-person autobiography, tracing his sense that his 19th-century education failed to prepare him for the dynamo-driven modern world.

entries.note: Indiana novelist and two-time Pulitzer winner; The Magnificent Ambersons traces a Gilded Age family's decline, and Alice Adams follows a status-anxious social climber.
Pulitzer-winning novelist of sweeping American sagas: So Big, the showboat saga Show Boat, and the Texas epic Giant.
Juvenile-fiction author of formulaic rags-to-riches tales like Ragged Dick, in which poor boys rise through pluck and lucky breaks.
Edward Bellamy's utopian novel in which Julian West wakes in the year 2000 to find Boston transformed into a socialist paradise.
John Luther Long's short story of the geisha Cho-Cho-San, abandoned by American officer Pinkerton and driven to suicide; source for Puccini's opera and a Belasco play.
Frank R. Stockton's story leaves unresolved whether a princess sends her lover to a tiger or a rival bride behind one of two doors.

sections.name: Modernist Fiction and the Lost Generation
sections.blurb: World War One's disillusioned American generation, many expatriated to Paris, traded realist narrative for psychological interiority, formal experiment, and satire of small-town and consumer culture.

entries.note: Iceberg-theory stylist of spare prose: The Sun Also Rises, A Farewell to Arms, and For Whom the Bell Tolls.

works.note: Novel of expatriate Jake Barnes, wounded and impotent, and Lady Brett Ashley's circle drifting from Paris cafes to the Pamplona bullfights.
Novel of ambulance driver Frederic Henry's affair with nurse Catherine Barkley amid the WWI Italian retreat from Caporetto; she dies in childbirth.
American lieutenant narrator of A Farewell to Arms who deserts the Italian army after the retreat from Caporetto.
Spanish Civil War novel of American dynamiter Robert Jordan's doomed love for Maria while blowing up a bridge with guerrillas.

works.topic: The Old Man and the Sea
works.answerline: The Old Man and the Sea
works.note: Novella of Santiago's three-day struggle to land a giant marlin, only for sharks to strip it before he reaches shore.
Hemingway's first major story collection, interleaving Nick Adams stories with terse italicized vignettes of war and violence.
Recurring Hemingway alter ego across linked short stories, from a wounded WWI veteran to a boy fishing alone in "Big Two-Hearted River."
Nick Adams returns from war to fish alone in the Michigan woods; understated prose masks unspoken postwar trauma.
Story told almost entirely in dialogue; a couple at a Spanish train station debate an unnamed "operation," implicitly an abortion.

works.topic: "The Short Happy Life of Francis Macomber"
works.answerline: "The Short Happy Life of Francis Macomber"
works.note: Story of a cowardly big-game hunter who briefly finds courage before being shot, ambiguously, by his wife Margot.
Dying writer Harry reflects on wasted talent on an African safari as gangrene sets in; opens with an epigraph about a frozen leopard.
Posthumous Paris memoir recalling the "lost generation" circle — Gertrude Stein, Fitzgerald, Ezra Pound — during Hemingway's 1920s expatriate years.

entries.note: Expatriate Paris salon host who coined "you are all a lost generation"; wrote The Autobiography of Alice B. Toklas and Tender Buttons.
Jazz Age chronicler: The Great Gatsby, Tender Is the Night, This Side of Paradise, and stories like "Bernice Bobs Her Hair" and "Babylon Revisited."

works.note: Fitzgerald's novel narrated by Nick Carraway: the green light, the eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg, and Myrtle's death under Daisy's driving are giveaways.
Fitzgerald's debut novel following Princeton student Amory Blaine's romantic disappointments and coming-of-age; ends "I know myself, but that is all-"
Novel of psychiatrist Dick Diver's marriage to wealthy patient Nicole, modeled on Zelda, unraveling amid the Riviera expatriate set.

works.topic: "The Diamond as Big as the Ritz"
works.answerline: "The Diamond as Big as the Ritz"
works.note: Story of John Unger visiting classmate Percy Washington's family, whose Montana mountain conceals a diamond the size of the Ritz; Kismine is the love interest.
Story of golf caddy Dexter Green's doomed pursuit of the wealthy, careless Judy Jones; an early sketch for Gatsby and Daisy.
Title character of "Bernice Bobs Her Hair," tricked by cousin Marjorie into a scandalous haircut before retaliating by shearing Marjorie's braids.
The Great Gatsby's careless object of Gatsby's longing, married to the philandering Tom; her voice is famously "full of money."
F. Scott's wife and model for Daisy Buchanan and Nicole Diver; wrote her own autobiographical novel, Save Me the Waltz, while institutionalized.

entries.note: Common-link surname spanning F. Scott and Zelda: their marriage, mutual literary borrowings, and Zelda's own novel Save Me the Waltz.
Zelda Fitzgerald's autobiographical novel of aspiring ballerina Alabama Beggs; written at Johns Hopkins while she was treated for schizophrenia.
Playwright-novelist of Our Town and The Skin of Our Teeth; also wrote The Matchmaker, source of Hello, Dolly!, and the novel The Bridge of San Luis Rey.

works.topic: The Bridge of San Luis Rey
works.answerline: The Bridge of San Luis Rey
works.note: Wilder's Pulitzer-winning novel investigating five deaths when a Peruvian rope bridge collapses, asking whether the victims were chosen by design.

entries.note: Chronicler of Depression-era California: The Grapes of Wrath, Of Mice and Men, East of Eden, and Cannery Row.

works.note: Novel of the Joads' migration from the Oklahoma Dust Bowl to California; Tom Joad's "I'll be there" speech and Rose of Sharon's final gesture close it.
Salinas Valley retelling of Cain and Abel across the Trask family; the Hebrew word timshel ("thou mayest") and villainous Cathy Ames anchor clues.
Novella of migrant workers George and the childlike, powerful Lennie, whose dream of a farm ends when George shoots him.
Episodic novel of Monterey's waterfront misfits, centered on marine biologist Doc and the vagrants of Mack and the boys.
Parable of pearl diver Kino, whose discovery of a great pearl brings greed and tragedy to his family.

entries.note: Modernist of the U.S.A. trilogy, known for its Newsreel and Camera Eye collage sections; also wrote Manhattan Transfer and Three Soldiers.
Darkly comic novelist of Miss Lonelyhearts, about an advice columnist, and The Day of the Locust, set amid a Hollywood mob riot.

works.note: Novella of a male advice columnist drowning in readers' letters of misery, tormented by his editor Shrike and Shrike's wife.
Novel of Hollywood's fringe dreamers converging in an apocalyptic riot after a premiere; the painting "The Burning of Los Angeles" frames it.

entries.note: First American Nobel literature laureate; satires of Midwestern conformity include Main Street, Babbitt, Arrowsmith, Elmer Gantry, and It Can't Happen Here.

works.note: Satire of conformist realtor George Babbitt's midlife crisis and half-hearted rebellion against small-city Zenith's boosterism.
Satire of reformer Carol Kennicott's stifled ambitions in the small Minnesota town of Gopher Prairie.
Novel following idealistic scientist Martin Arrowsmith's career from small-town doctor to bacteriologist battling a Caribbean plague.
Satire of a hypocritical, womanizing evangelist preacher; dedicated to Lewis's friend H. L. Mencken.
Novel imagining a fascist demagogue, Buzz Windrip, elected U.S. president and installing an authoritarian regime.

entries.note: Baltimore journalist-critic and "Sage of Baltimore" who covered the Scopes trial, wrote The American Language, and was Elmer Gantry's dedicatee.
Story-cycle modernist best known for Winesburg, Ohio's portrait of small-town "grotesques."

works.note: Linked story cycle about small-town "grotesques," framed by "The Book of the Grotesque" and unified by young reporter George Willard; "Hands" opens it.
Young reporter who links Winesburg, Ohio's stories, observing the town's isolated "grotesques" before leaving for the city.
Winesburg, Ohio's twitchy-handed former teacher, driven from a Pennsylvania town by a false molestation accusation; his story opens the cycle ("Hands").

entries.note: New Yorker humorist and cartoonist best known for the daydreaming title character of "The Secret Life of Walter Mitty."

works.note: Henpecked daydreamer of Thurber's story who imagines himself a Navy pilot, surgeon, and marksman amid mundane errands; catchphrase "pocketa-pocketa-pocketa."

entries.note: Common-link 1920s New York "Vicious Circle" of wits lunching at the Algonquin Hotel: Dorothy Parker, Robert Benchley, Alexander Woollcott, Robert Sherwood, George S. Kaufman.
Critic who edited Fitzgerald's The Crack-Up and unfinished The Last Tycoon; wrote Axel's Castle and Patriotic Gore, and was Mary McCarthy's second husband.
Expatriate Paris novelist of explicit, autobiographical fiction; Tropic of Cancer, banned for decades in the U.S., made his name.

works.note: Autobiographical novel of Miller's impoverished, sexually frank life among Paris expatriates; banned as obscene in the U.S. until 1961.

entries.note: Diarist and Henry Miller's lover, fictionalized as Tania in Tropic of Cancer; wrote erotica (Delta of Venus, Little Birds) and the novel sequence Cities of the Interior.
Provincetown Players writer and Paris expatriate best known for Nightwood, a modernist novel of lesbian love and Greenwich Village decline.

works.note: Barnes's novel of Robin Vote's relationships with Nora Flood and Jenny Petherbridge, and her marriage to Baron Volkbein; ends with Robin barking at a dog.

entries.note: Humorist of baseball vernacular fiction (You Know Me Al) and the story "Haircut," about a small-town prankster's revenge killing.
Henry Roth's modernist novel of young immigrant David Schearl's Lower East Side childhood, ending with a near-electrocution on subway tracks.
Narrative technique rendering a character's unbroken flow of thought; associated with Faulkner's The Sound and the Fury among American modernists.

entries.topic: Let Us Now Praise Famous Men
entries.answerline: Let Us Now Praise Famous Men
entries.note: James Agee's text, with Walker Evans's photographs, documenting Alabama sharecropper families (the pseudonymous Gudgers, Woods, and Ricketts) during the Depression.

sections.name: Modernist Poetry
sections.blurb: The early twentieth century's break from Victorian verse, from Pound's Imagist manifesto and H.D.'s crystalline lyrics through the high modernism of Eliot, Stevens, and Moore to Frost's plainspoken New England voice.

entries.note: Imagist impresario who edited The Waste Land: "In a Station of the Metro," Hugh Selwyn Mauberley, and The Cantos; his fascist broadcasts and confinement at St. Elizabeths recur.

works.note: Pound's sprawling, unfinished 116-plus-section epic, opening with a translation of Odysseus's descent to the underworld; includes the usura canto and the Pisan Cantos, written in a detention camp.

entries.note: Pound-launched movement demanding "direct treatment of the thing"; anthologized in Des Imagistes and later led by Amy Lowell ("Amygism"); H.D. and "In a Station of the Metro" are stock clues.
Pen name of Hilda Doolittle, signed "H.D. Imagiste" by Pound; crystalline lyrics like "Oread" and "Sea Rose" plus the later epics Trilogy and Helen in Egypt appear.

works.note: H.D.'s Madrigal-cycle autobiographical novel and its title flower, linked to Williams's "Asphodel, That Greeny Flower" and the underworld.

entries.note: Boston Imagist poet-critic who succeeded Pound as movement leader ("Amygism"); pioneered free-verse "polyphonic prose."

works.note: Lowell poem of a woman in a formal garden mourning her fiancé's death in war; ends "Christ! What are patterns for?"

entries.note: Rutherford physician-poet of "The Red Wheelbarrow," "This Is Just to Say," Spring and All, and the epic Paterson; his slogan was "no ideas but in things."

works.note: Williams's late long poem addressed to his wife Flossie, recalling Helen of Troy and learning "there were flowers also in hell."
Williams's epic poem-in-prose of the New Jersey city and the Passaic Falls, embodying his credo "no ideas but in things."
Williams's five-word poetic credo from Paterson's preface, repeated in the poem after the command "Say it!"
Sixteen-word imagist poem: "so much depends / upon / a red wheel / barrow," glazed with rainwater beside white chickens.

entries.note: Precise syllabic-verse modernist; "Poetry" ("imaginary gardens with real toads in them"), "The Fish," and her tricorn hat and Brooklyn Dodgers fandom are clues.

works.note: Moore's poem opening "I, too, dislike it"; her 1967 revision cut it to just three lines, ending on "a place for the genuine."
Moore poem of a church spire re-gilded in a seaside town amid stranded whales; opens "Dürer would have seen a reason for living in a town like this."

entries.note: Hartford insurance executive; "The Emperor of Ice-Cream," "Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird," "Anecdote of the Jar," "Sunday Morning," and the collection Harmonium recur constantly.

works.note: Stevens poem in which a woman lounging with coffee and oranges skips church; ends with "casual flocks of pigeons" sinking "downward to darkness, on extended wings."
Stevens's 1923 debut collection, containing "Sunday Morning," "The Emperor of Ice-Cream," and "Peter Quince at the Clavier."
Stevens poem set at a wake, commanding "Let be be finale of seem" and declaring ice cream "the only emperor."
Stevens poem retelling Susanna and the Elders through a musician-narrator; declares "Music is feeling, then, not sound."

works.topic: "Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird"
works.answerline: "Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird"
works.note: Stevens's thirteen-stanza sequence of aphoristic vignettes, each a distinct perspective on the title bird.
Ten-canto title poem of Stevens's last original collection, opening "Farewell to an idea" and meditating on the Northern Lights.

works.topic: "The Idea of Order at Key West"
works.answerline: "The Idea of Order at Key West"
works.note: Stevens poem of a woman singing by the sea while the speaker asks Ramon Fernandez about the "maker's rage to order."

entries.note: St. Louis-born, British-naturalized modernist of "The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock," The Waste Land, and Four Quartets.

works.note: Eliot's four-part early poem of dingy urban imagery, opening at "six o'clock" on a winter evening; ends "gathering fuel in vacant lots."
Eliot's recurring "apeneck" antihero of "Sweeney Erect," "Sweeney Among the Nightingales," and the unfinished verse drama Sweeney Agonistes.

entries.note: New England poet of "The Road Not Taken," "Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening," "Mending Wall," "Birches," and "Fire and Ice"; recited at Kennedy's inauguration.

works.note: Frost dialogue in which Warren and Mary debate taking back the dying hired man Silas; "home is the place where... they have to take you in."
Frost poem of neighbors annually repairing a stone wall; the speaker questions it, but the neighbor insists "good fences make good neighbors."
Frost poem imagining a boy swinging on birch trees bent by ice storms; the speaker wishes to "get away from earth awhile."
Frost poem of a husband and wife's icy dialogue after burying their dead child; ends with his threat "I'll follow and bring you back by force."
Frost poem, titled from Macbeth's "tomorrow and tomorrow" speech, of a boy who bleeds to death after a buzz saw severs his hand.
Frost poem beginning "The land was ours before we were the land's"; recited from memory at JFK's 1961 inauguration.
Frost's second collection (1914), containing "Mending Wall," "The Death of the Hired Man," and "Home Burial"; established his reputation.
Late Frost poem guiding the reader back to a ruined farmhouse and playhouse; ends "Drink and be whole again beyond confusion."

works.topic: "Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening"
works.answerline: "Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening"
works.note: Frost poem of a traveler pausing by "the darkest evening of the year"; ends with "miles to go before I sleep."

entries.note: Poet of fictional Tilbury Town: "Richard Cory," who "went home and put a bullet through his head," plus "Miniver Cheevy" and "Mr. Flood's Party."

works.note: Robinson poem of a woman's fear-driven, loveless marriage, its title Greek for "Love the Tyrant."
Robinson poem in which a mysterious voice at "the western gate" summons the grieving title man toward his dead love.
Robinson poem of a man who romanticizes the medieval past and Medici Florence, resents modernity, and "coughed, and called it fate."
Robinson's fictional New England town, based on his native Gardiner, Maine; setting for "Miniver Cheevy," "Richard Cory," and other character portraits.

entries.note: Poet of the fictional Illinois town Spoon River, whose dead residents narrate their lives as verse epitaphs in Spoon River Anthology.

works.note: Masters's collection of free-verse epitaphs spoken by Spoon River's dead, opening "All, all are sleeping on the hill."
Masters's fictional Midwestern town, home to Anne Rutledge, Lucinda Matlock, and Jonathan Swift Somers's mock-epic within Spoon River Anthology.

entries.note: Performer of chanted "singing poetry": "The Congo," "General William Booth Enters into Heaven," and "Abraham Lincoln Walks at Midnight"; he discovered Langston Hughes waiting tables.
Chicago poet of "Chicago" ("City of the Big Shoulders") and "Fog" ("comes on little cat feet"); also wrote a multivolume Lincoln biography.
Author of the Pulitzer-winning Civil War verse epic John Brown's Body and the story "The Devil and Daniel Webster," in which Webster out-argues Mr. Scratch before a jury of the damned.
Poet-Librarian of Congress; "Ars Poetica" ("A poem should not mean / But be"), the Pulitzer epic Conquistador, and the Job-based verse play J.B.
Visionary poet of The Bridge, addressed to the Brooklyn Bridge, and White Buildings; died jumping from the steamship Orizaba while returning from Mexico.

works.note: Crane's epic poem addressed to the Brooklyn Bridge in its "Proem," with sections including "Powhatan's Daughter" and "Cape Hatteras."

entries.note: Lowercase typographical experimenter: "anyone lived in a pretty how town," "in Just-," "l(a," and the World War I internment memoir The Enormous Room.

works.topic: "i sing of olaf glad and big"
works.answerline: "i sing of olaf glad and big"
works.note: Cummings poem of a conscientious objector tortured by the army who declares "there is some shit I will not eat."
Typographical convention Cummings's poetry and penname largely eschew, alongside conventional punctuation and syntax.

entries.note: Objectivist poet who edited Poetry's 1931 "Objectivists" issue; spent five decades on his book-length long poem "A."

works.note: Zukofsky's book-length long poem written over five decades, its twenty-four sections corresponding to the hours of the day.

entries.note: California poet who built Tor House and Hawk Tower near Carmel; his "inhumanist" narrative poems include "Roan Stallion."
Sonneteer of Greenwich Village bohemia: "First Fig" ("My candle burns at both ends"), "Renascence," and the Pulitzer-winning The Ballad of the Harp-Weaver.

works.note: Millay's breakthrough long poem, written as a teenager, of a mystical near-burial vision that expands the speaker's sense of infinity.

entries.note: Algonquin Round Table wit; the story "Big Blonde," the poem "Résumé" ("you might as well live"), and her acid Constant Reader reviews fill questions.
Light-verse humorist of mismatched comic rhymes and couplets, such as "Candy / Is dandy / But liquor / Is quicker."
British-born poet who became a U.S. citizen and is asked as American literature: "Musée des Beaux Arts," "Funeral Blues," "The Unknown Citizen," and The Age of Anxiety.

works.note: Auden's poem written "in one of the dives / on Fifty-second Street" at the outbreak of World War II; "We must love one another or die" is the famous line he later disowned.

sections.name: Southern Literature
sections.blurb: The South's Gothic decay and grotesque grace, from Faulkner's Yoknapatawpha and O'Connor's dark parables through the Fugitive-Agrarian critics to Capote, Percy, and Styron.

entries.note: Nobel laureate of fictional Yoknapatawpha County: The Sound and the Fury, As I Lay Dying, Absalom, Absalom!, Light in August, and "A Rose for Emily."

works.note: Compson family novel in four sections — Benjy's, Quentin's (ending in suicide at Harvard), Jason's, and Dilsey's; the title comes from Macbeth.
Yoknapatawpha family whose decline is chronicled across The Sound and the Fury's four narrators: Quentin, Caddy, Jason, and Benjy.
Mentally disabled Compson sibling whose nonlinear, achronological section opens The Sound and the Fury.
Compson sister whose muddy drawers and later promiscuity haunt her brothers Quentin and Benjy throughout The Sound and the Fury.
Compson son who narrates a section of The Sound and the Fury before drowning himself, and reconstructs Sutpen's story in Absalom, Absalom!
Bundren family's multi-narrator journey to bury matriarch Addie in Jefferson; Darl burns a barn and is committed to an asylum.
Temple Drake is abducted and raped with a corncob by the impotent gangster Popeye, then perjures herself at Lee Goodwin's trial.
Sequel to Sanctuary in which Temple Stevens's nurse Nancy Mannigoe kills her infant daughter to save the family.
Joe Christmas, a man of ambiguous race, is castrated and killed by Percy Grimm while Lena Grove searches for her child's father.
Thomas Sutpen's ruthless design to found a dynasty collapses when son Henry kills half-brother Charles Bon; reconstructed by Quentin and Shreve.
Poor white arriviste whose dynastic "design" in Absalom, Absalom! unravels over his sons Henry and the part-Black Charles Bon.
Shreve McCannon's question to Quentin Compson closing Absalom, Absalom!, answered by Quentin's repeated "I don't hate it."
Story collection following the McCaslin family across generations, including Ike McCaslin's repudiation of his inheritance and the hunt for Old Ben.
Ike McCaslin, guided by Sam Fathers and the dog Lion, hunts the legendary bear Old Ben; collected in Go Down, Moses.
Lucas Beauchamp, falsely accused of murder, is saved from a lynch mob by teenager Chick Mallison's investigation.
Emily Grierson keeps the poisoned corpse of her lover Homer Barron in her bed for decades; narrated by the town's collective "we."
Young Sarty Snopes must decide whether to warn a family that his arsonist father Abner Snopes plans to burn their barn.
Miss Minnie Cooper's rumored assault leads a mob to lynch Will Mayes despite barber Henry Hawkshaw's attempt to stop it.

entries.note: Georgia Catholic master of the grotesque: "A Good Man Is Hard to Find" with The Misfit, "Good Country People," Wise Blood, and The Violent Bear It Away.

works.topic: "A Good Man Is Hard to Find"
works.answerline: "A Good Man is Hard to Find"
works.note: Title story of O'Connor's debut collection; a family's Florida road trip ends when the escaped convict Misfit murders them one by one.
Unnamed protagonist of "A Good Man Is Hard to Find" whose lie about a hidden plantation causes the wreck that delivers her family to the Misfit.
Escaped convict who muses on Jesus and grace before shooting the grandmother and her family in "A Good Man Is Hard to Find."
Julian's racist mother collapses after a Black woman wearing her same hat strikes her at a bus stop; title from Teilhard de Chardin.
Ruby Turpin is called a "wart hog from hell" by the enraged Mary Grace in a doctor's waiting room, prompting a vision of ascending souls.
O.E. Parker covers his back with a Byzantine Christ tattoo to please his fundamentalist wife Sarah Ruth, who beats him for it.

works.topic: "The Life You Save May Be Your Own"
works.answerline: "The Life You Save May Be Your Own"
works.note: Drifter Tom T. Shiftlet marries, then abandons, the deaf-mute Lucynell Crater for a car.
Hazel Motes preaches a nihilistic "Church Without Christ" before blinding himself in penance.
Young Francis Marion Tarwater resists his prophetic calling to baptize his cousin Bishop, whom he ultimately drowns.

entries.note: Georgia novelist of lonely misfits: The Heart Is a Lonely Hunter, The Member of the Wedding with Frankie Addams, and The Ballad of the Sad Café.

works.topic: The Heart Is a Lonely Hunter
works.answerline: The Heart is a Lonely Hunter
works.note: McCullers's debut about deaf-mute John Singer, confidant to Mick Kelly, Dr. Copeland, Jake Blount, and Biff Brannon; Singer shoots himself after his friend Antonapoulos dies.

works.topic: The Ballad of the Sad Café
works.answerline: The Ballad of the Sad Cafe
works.note: Love triangle in a rural Georgia café between the giant Miss Amelia Evans, hunchbacked Cousin Lymon, and her ex-husband Marvin Macy.
Twelve-year-old Frankie Addams, renaming herself F. Jasmine, longs to join her brother's wedding, confiding in cook Berenice Sadie Brown.

entries.note: Mississippi story writer: "A Worn Path" with Phoenix Jackson, "Why I Live at the P.O.," the Pulitzer-winning The Optimist's Daughter, and the memoir One Writer's Beginnings.

works.note: Elderly Phoenix Jackson walks a long path into Natchez to fetch throat medicine for her grandson.
Unnamed narrator's comic feud with her sister Stella-Rondo drives her to move into the small-town post office.
Laurel McKelva returns to Mississippi after her father Judge McKelva's death and clashes with his young second wife, Fay; won the Pulitzer.
Fairy-tale-style novella of planter's daughter Rosamond and the bandit Jamie Lockhart in frontier Natchez Trace Mississippi.

entries.note: The only writer with Pulitzers in both fiction and poetry, and the first official U.S. Poet Laureate; All the King's Men and the Fugitive-Agrarian circle supply the clues.

works.note: Louisiana demagogue Willie Stark's rise and fall, narrated by aide Jack Burden, whose historical digging drives Judge Irwin to suicide; loosely based on Huey Long.
Huey Long-style Louisiana governor whose corrupt rise and assassination are narrated by aide Jack Burden in All the King's Men.

entries.note: Fugitive-Agrarian poet and critic married to Caroline Gordon; best known for his meditation on Confederate war dead outside a cemetery gate.

works.note: Speaker standing at a Confederate cemetery gate wrestles with modern disconnection from the heroic, mythologized Southern past.

entries.note: Fugitive-Agrarian poet-critic at Vanderbilt whose textbook The New Criticism named that movement; mentored fellow Fugitives Allen Tate and Robert Penn Warren.
Mid-20th-century critical movement championing close reading of texts in isolation from biography; named by Ransom, joined by Cleanth Brooks and W.K. Wimsatt's "intentional fallacy."
Texas-born writer of Pale Horse, Pale Rider, "The Jilting of Granny Weatherall," the Miranda stories, and the allegorical ocean-liner novel Ship of Fools.

works.note: Newspaperwoman Miranda nearly dies of influenza during World War I while her soldier lover Adam Barclay dies of the same pandemic.

entries.note: Author of the "nonfiction novel" In Cold Blood, about the Clutter family murders, plus Breakfast at Tiffany's with Holly Golightly and Other Voices, Other Rooms.

works.note: Novella narrated by an unnamed writer about his glamorous, husband-hunting neighbor's escapades in 1940s New York.
"Nonfiction novel" reconstructing killers Perry Smith and Dick Hickock's 1959 murder of the Clutter family in Holcomb, Kansas.
Café-society "American geisha" who befriends the narrator of Breakfast at Tiffany's while chasing a rich husband.

entries.note: John Kennedy Toole's posthumous Pulitzer-winning New Orleans comedy starring hot-dog vendor Ignatius J. Reilly, devotee of Boethius; published through Walker Percy's advocacy.
Louisiana novelist of alienated searchers; The Moviegoer's Binx Bolling embodies his philosophical "malaise," and he championed A Confederacy of Dunces into print.

works.note: New Orleans stockbroker Binx Bolling searches for authenticity amid his "malaise" and cousin Kate's suicidal despair; won the National Book Award.

entries.note: Alabama author of To Kill a Mockingbird; its sequel/first draft Go Set a Watchman controversially recast Atticus Finch as a segregationist.

works.note: Adult Jean Louise "Scout" Finch returns to Maycomb and is disillusioned to find her father Atticus sympathetic to segregation.

entries.note: Margaret Mitchell's Pulitzer-winning Civil War epic of Scarlett O'Hara's survival at Tara plantation and her stormy romance with Rhett Butler.
William Styron's Brooklyn-set novel narrated by Stingo; Auschwitz survivor Sophie Zawistowski's fatal wartime choice and her volatile lover Nathan Landau.
Leader of an 1831 Virginia slave revolt whose "confessions" to lawyer Thomas Gray inspired William Styron's controversial novel The Confessions of Nat Turner.

sections.name: African American Literature: Wheatley to the Harlem Renaissance
sections.blurb: African American letters from Phillis Wheatley's colonial-era poetry through slavery-era narratives to the 1920s Harlem Renaissance's flowering of poetry and fiction.

entries.note: Enslaved poet from Long Island whose 1760 "An Evening Thought" was the first poem published by an African American; also wrote "An Address to Miss Phillis Wheatley."
African-born enslaved poet of revolutionary Boston, first African American to publish a poetry book; wrote "On Being Brought from Africa to America."
Escaped slave, orator, and editor of The North Star; his 1845 Narrative, the fight with slave-breaker Covey, and "What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?" are the clues.
Common-link genre spanning Douglass, Harriet Jacobs's Incidents, Olaudah Equiano, and Solomon Northup, often narrated or published under a pseudonym while still enslaved.

entries.topic: Incidents in the Life of a Slave Girl
entries.answerline: Incidents in the Life of a Slave Girl
entries.note: Harriet Jacobs's narrative, published as "Linda Brent," of seven years hiding in her grandmother's garret to escape the predatory Dr. Flint.
Sociologist-activist and NAACP co-founder; The Souls of Black Folk introduced "double consciousness" and "the Veil," and his fiction included the story "The Comet."

works.note: Du Bois's 1903 essay collection on "double consciousness," "the color line," and the "sorrow songs," including the story "Of the Coming of John."

entries.note: Author of The Conjure Woman and The House Behind the Cedars, dramatizing Reconstruction-era racial passing and folk conjure tales.

works.note: Novel of Rena Walden, who passes as white to become George Tryon's "queen of love and beauty" until he learns her race and breaks their engagement.

entries.note: First nationally famous Black American poet; "We Wear the Mask" and "Sympathy" ("I know why the caged bird sings") anchor nearly every question.

works.note: Poem whose title object "grins and lies, hides our cheeks and shades our eyes" while masking the speaker's true suffering from the world.
Poem of a caged bird who sings "a prayer... from his heart's deep core"; its refrain gave Maya Angelou her memoir title, I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings.
Married surname shared by the poet and his first wife, Alice Dunbar-Nelson (born Alice Ruth Moore), author of Violets and Other Tales.

entries.note: Writer of "Lift Every Voice and Sing," the novel The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man, and the sermon-poems of God's Trombones; also an NAACP leader and diplomat.

works.note: Unnamed narrator who passes as white after witnessing a lynching in Georgia, ultimately confessing he sold his "birthright for a mess of pottage."

entries.note: The 1920s flowering of Black art centered in upper Manhattan: Hughes, Hurston, McKay, Countee Cullen, and Alain Locke's anthology The New Negro are the standard clues.
Poet laureate of the Harlem Renaissance: "The Negro Speaks of Rivers," "Harlem," "I, Too," "The Weary Blues," and the Jesse B. Semple ("Simple") stories.

works.note: Poem of a pianist "swaying to and fro on his rickety stool," playing a "drowsy syncopated tune" on Lenox Avenue until he sleeps "like a rock."
Poem addressed to a white instructor; the speaker, the "only colored student" in his class, insists "that's American" of what the two share.

entries.note: Common-link musical genre clued through Hughes's poetry and Baldwin's story "Sonny's Blues," typically via a "drowsy syncopated tune" and the AAB stanza form.
Anthropologist-novelist from Eatonville, Florida: Their Eyes Were Watching God, the folklore collection Mules and Men, and the essay "How It Feels to Be Colored Me."

works.note: Hurston's novel of Janie Crawford's three marriages; the pear tree, the mule of Eatonville, the hurricane, and the shooting of rabid Tea Cake are the standard clues.
Short story of Delia Jones, whose washing "sweat" pays for her house while husband Sykes dies from a rattlesnake he planted to kill her.
Story set in Eatonville where Joe pays Missie May with a fake gold coin after catching her affair with ice-cream parlor owner Otis Slemmons.
Hurston's all-Black hometown, setting of Their Eyes Were Watching God and "The Gilded Six-Bits" and subject of "How It Feels to Be Colored Me."
Janie's much-younger third husband in Their Eyes Were Watching God, shot by her in self-defense after a rabid dog bite drives him mad.

entries.note: Jamaican-born Harlem Renaissance poet of the defiant sonnet "If We Must Die" and "America" ("this cultured hell that tests my youth"), plus the novel Home to Harlem.

works.note: McKay's island homeland, evoked in his dialect collection Songs of Jamaica and the novel Banana Bottom, set in its title village.
Sonnet urging "Kinsmen" to face "the murderous, cowardly pack," fighting back rather than dying "like hogs...in an inglorious spot."

entries.note: Harlem Renaissance poet of the collection Color and its sonnet "Yet Do I Marvel"; "Incident" recalls a childhood racial slur suffered on a trip to Baltimore.

works.note: Sonnet on God's inscrutable ways, from Tantalus to the blind mole, before the "curious thing" of making "a poet black, and bid him sing."

entries.note: Harlem Renaissance author of Cane, mixing poetry, prose, and drama; grandson of Louisiana governor P. B. S. Pinchback, later a disciple of George Gurdjieff.

works.note: 1923 collection of vignettes, poems, and the closing play "Kabnis," opening with "Karintha" and including "Georgia Dusk" and "Blood-Burning Moon."

entries.note: Harlem Renaissance novelist of Passing and Quicksand, exploring racial ambiguity and light-skinned Black women caught between white and Black worlds.

works.note: Novel of Irene Redfield and the racially ambiguous Clare Kendry, who dies falling from a window after her white husband learns she is Black.
Novel of Helga Crane, half-Danish and half-Black, who drifts from the Tuskegee-like school Naxos through Chicago, Harlem, and Copenhagen before marrying a rural minister.

sections.name: Postwar African American Literature
sections.blurb: From Richard Wright's naturalist protest fiction and the essays of Baldwin and Ellison through Toni Morrison's Nobel-winning novels to the poets, critics, and novelists carrying that tradition forward today.

entries.note: Naturalist of Black protest fiction: Native Son, the memoir Black Boy, and the story collection Uncle Tom's Children with "Big Boy Leaves Home."

works.note: Wright's novel of Bigger Thomas, who accidentally smothers Mary Dalton in Chicago and is defended by Boris Max; its three books are Fear, Flight, and Fate.
Fearful protagonist who kills Mary Dalton and his girlfriend Bessie Mears in Native Son; defended by lawyer Boris Max, sentenced to death.
Wright's memoir of poverty and violence in Jim Crow Mississippi and Memphis; its long-suppressed second part, American Hunger, covers Chicago's Communist Party.

entries.note: Author of Invisible Man and the essay collection Shadow and Act; the posthumous novel Juneteenth was assembled from decades of drafts.

works.note: 1952 novel of an unnamed narrator's journey from a Southern "battle royal" through the Brotherhood in Harlem, ending underground in a room lit by 1,369 bulbs.

entries.note: Essayist-novelist of Go Tell It on the Mountain, Giovanni's Room, and The Fire Next Time; the story "Sonny's Blues" is a stock short-fiction clue.

works.topic: Go Tell It on the Mountain
works.answerline: Go Tell It on the Mountain
works.note: Semi-autobiographical novel of John Grimes's religious awakening in a Harlem church on his fourteenth birthday; his abusive stepfather is preacher Gabriel.
Novel of the American David's Paris affair with the Italian bartender Giovanni, executed for murder, while his fiancee Hella is away in Spain.
Two-part essay collection: "My Dungeon Shook," a letter to Baldwin's nephew, and "Down at the Cross," recounting a dinner with Elijah Muhammad.
Story of a Harlem algebra teacher whose heroin-addicted jazz-pianist brother Sonny plays with Creole's band in a Greenwich Village club.
Title essay of Baldwin's 1955 collection; recalls his father's funeral coinciding with the 1943 Harlem race riot and a jug of water thrown at a waitress.

entries.note: First Black Pulitzer winner, for Annie Allen; "We Real Cool" ("We / Die soon") and the Chicago world of A Street in Bronzeville dominate clues.

works.note: Eight-line poem on "the pool players, Seven at the Golden Shovel," each line but the last ending in "We"; inspired Terrance Hayes's golden shovel form.

entries.note: First Black poet to hold the Library of Congress poetry consultantship; "Those Winter Sundays" ("love's austere and lonely offices") and the slave-ship poem "Middle Passage" are the clues.

works.note: Poem on a father who rose early to heat the house in "blueblack cold," his "love's austere and lonely offices" going unthanked.

entries.note: Black Arts Movement founder, formerly LeRoi Jones: the play Dutchman, in which Lula murders Clay on a subway, and the controversial poem "Somebody Blew Up America."
Black Arts-adjacent postmodern satirist; coined "Neo-HooDooism" and the "neo-slave narrative" (Flight to Canada), with the houngan Papa LaBas recurring across several novels.

works.note: Novel of Papa LaBas battling the secret Wallflower Order to stop "Jes Grew," a virus spreading 1920s Black cultural and musical expression.

entries.note: Nobel laureate whose Beloved, Song of Solomon, The Bluest Eye, Sula, and Jazz make her the most-asked postwar American novelist.

works.note: Morrison's novel of Sethe, haunted at 124 Bluestone Road by the daughter she killed rather than return to slavery; Paul D, Denver, and the Sweet Home plantation are stock clues.
Beloved's protagonist, modeled on Margaret Garner; kills her daughter with a saw rather than let the Schoolteacher return them to the Sweet Home plantation.
Beloved's haunted Cincinnati address, "spiteful" and "full of a baby's venom," which scares off Sethe's sons Howard and Buglar.
Beloved's former Sweet Home fellow slave who becomes Sethe's lover at 124 and drives out the house's ghost.
Morrison's novel of Milkman Dead's journey south to trace the flying African Solomon; his aunt Pilate, born without a navel, and the insurance agent's opening leap are clues.
Common-link nickname: Macon "Milkman" Dead III, who searches for Pilate's gold in Song of Solomon, and the title stalker of Anna Burns's Troubles-era Booker winner Milkman.
Morrison's first novel: Pecola Breedlove prays for blue eyes in Lorain, Ohio; partly narrated by Claudia MacTeer and framed by a distorted Dick-and-Jane primer.
Novel of Sula Peace and Nel Wright's friendship in the Bottom; Shadrack's yearly "National Suicide Day" and the drowning of Chicken Little recur.
Title character who drowns Chicken Little as a child, sleeps with Nel's husband Jude, and returns to Medallion amid a "plague of robins."

entries.note: Author of The Color Purple; the story "Everyday Use," the essays of In Search of Our Mothers' Gardens, and her role reviving Hurston's reputation also appear.

works.note: Walker's epistolary Pulitzer winner: Celie's letters to God and from her missionary sister Nettie in Africa, with blues singer Shug Avery and the abusive Mister.

entries.note: Self-described "Black, lesbian, mother, warrior, poet"; the essay "The Master's Tools Will Never Dismantle the Master's House," The Cancer Journals, and the "biomythography" Zami.
Memoirist-poet of I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings; "Still I Rise," "Phenomenal Woman," and reading "On the Pulse of Morning" at Clinton's inauguration recur.

works.topic: I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings
works.answerline: I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings
works.note: First of Angelou's seven memoirs: her Stamps, Arkansas childhood, rape by Mr. Freeman that leaves her mute, and becoming San Francisco's first Black streetcar conductor.
Inaugural poem urging listeners to "lift up your eyes" and "lift up your hearts," spoken by "A Rock, A River, A Tree"; read at Clinton's 1993 inauguration.

entries.note: Black Arts poet of "Nikki-Rosa" and "Knoxville, Tennessee"; delivered the "We Are Virginia Tech" chant-poem after the 2007 campus shooting.
Poet of Lighthead, winner of the 2010 National Book Award, and American Sonnets for My Past and Future Assassin; invented the golden shovel form from Brooks's "We Real Cool."
Poet-essayist of the book-length "American Lyric" Citizen, addressing Serena Williams and Trayvon Martin in the second person; also wrote Just Us.
Afrofuturist author of Kindred and the Earthseed novels Parable of the Sower and Parable of the Talents; also wrote the novella "Bloodchild."

works.note: Neo-slave narrative in which writer Dana time-travels from 1970s Los Angeles to save her slaveholding ancestor Rufus Weylin on a Maryland plantation.

entries.note: Back-to-back Pulitzer winner for The Underground Railroad, which makes the railroad literal, and The Nickel Boys, based on the Dozier School; earlier wrote The Intuitionist and Zone One.

works.note: Debut novel of Lila Mae Watson, the first Black female elevator inspector, whose intuitionist school of inspection rivals the rationalist Empiricists.

entries.note: Mississippi novelist of Salvage the Bones and the National Book Award-winning Sing, Unburied, Sing; also edited the essay collection The Fire This Time.

works.note: Novel of pregnant teenager Esch Batiste and her brother Skeetah's prize pit bull China, set around Hurricane Katrina's landfall.

entries.note: Metafictional satirist of Erasure, whose academic Thelonious "Monk" Ellison pens the parody novel "My Pafology"; also wrote Dr. No and the Huck Finn retelling James.
Author of the memoir The Color of Water, the John Brown novel The Good Lord Bird, and Deacon King Kong, set in a Brooklyn housing project.
Atlantic writer of Between the World and Me, a letter to his son Samori, and the essay "The Case for Reparations"; also wrote the novel The Water Dancer.
Paul Beatty's 2016 Booker winner: the narrator tries reintroducing segregation to the disincorporated Los Angeles ghetto of Dickens and keeps Hominy as a slave.
Ernest Gaines's novel of schoolteacher Grant Wiggins mentoring the wrongly condemned Jefferson, urged to die "like a man," not a "hog."
Feminist theorist, pen name lowercase to honor her great-grandmother; wrote Ain't I a Woman?, Teaching to Transgress, and All About Love.
Harvard critic of The Signifying Monkey, which theorizes Black "double-voiced" texts via the trickster figure Esu-Elegbara; co-edited the Norton Anthology of African-American Literature.

sections.name: American Drama
sections.blurb: American drama's twentieth century, from O'Neill's tragic naturalism through Group Theatre social realism and the Williams-Miller golden age to August Wilson's century-spanning Pittsburgh Cycle.

entries.note: America's first great playwright and its only dramatist Nobel laureate: Long Day's Journey into Night, The Iceman Cometh, The Hairy Ape, Desire Under the Elms, and Mourning Becomes Electra.

works.note: O'Neill play set in Harry Hope's saloon, where salesman Hickey urges the regulars to abandon their "pipe dreams"; he is revealed to have murdered his wife Evelyn.
Agoraphobic Harry Hope's Raines-Law bar, setting of The Iceman Cometh, where Hickey confronts the regulars' "pipe dreams" and Don Parritt leaps to his death.
Charismatic salesman in The Iceman Cometh who urges Harry Hope's regulars to abandon their "pipe dreams," then confesses to murdering his wife Evelyn.
O'Neill's posthumously produced autobiographical play: miserly actor James Tyrone, his morphine-addicted wife Mary, and sons Jamie and consumptive Edmund across one day in Connecticut.
Morphine-addicted matriarch of Long Day's Journey into Night; the family maid Cathleen and an offstage foghorn accompany her retreat into the past.
Recurring O'Neill motif: shrouds Anna Christie's coastal setting and keeps the morphine-addicted Mary Tyrone awake via foghorn in Long Day's Journey into Night.
O'Neill play of stoker Yank, rejected by Mildred Douglas and the IWW alike, who is crushed to death after freeing a gorilla from its cage.
O'Neill play in which ex-Pullman porter Brutus Jones, ruling a Caribbean island, flees into the forest pursued by "Little Formless Fears" and is killed by a silver bullet.
O'Neill play in which the title former prostitute reunites with her father, barge captain Chris Christopherson, and falls for the Irish stoker Mat Burke.
O'Neill's Pulitzer-winning play of Nina Leeds, whose soliloquies interrupt the dialogue; she marries Sam Evans but conceives her son with Dr. Ned Darrell.
O'Neill's Hippolytus-inspired tragedy: young wife Abbie Putnam seduces stepson Eben Cabot on the family farm, then smothers their child to prove her love.
O'Neill's only comedy: Richard Miller's Fourth of July coming-of-age in Connecticut with sweetheart Muriel McComber; the title quotes the Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam.

entries.note: Group Theatre social dramatist of the Depression: Waiting for Lefty, Awake and Sing!, and Golden Boy, about violinist-turned-boxer Joe Bonaparte.
