works.note: Odets's agitprop one-act at a taxi-union meeting awaiting organizer Lefty Costello, who is found shot dead; it ends with the audience-rousing cry of "Strike!"
Odets's Depression-era Bronx family drama: the Marxist grandfather Jacob commits suicide so his insurance money can free grandson Ralph; daughter Hennie is pregnant.
Odets's Group Theatre hit: violinist Joe Bonaparte gives up music for boxing and Lorna Moon, then dies crashing his car after a fatal bout.

entries.note: Playwright of The Little Foxes, The Children's Hour, and Watch on the Rhine; her memoir Pentimento and her defiant letter to HUAC also appear as clues.

works.note: Hellman's play about the grasping Hubbard family of the post-Reconstruction South; Regina Giddens lets her husband Horace die by withholding his heart medicine.
Hellman play in which student Mary Tilford's lie that teachers Karen and Martha are lovers destroys their school; Martha confesses her feelings and kills herself.
Hellman play in which anti-fascist Kurt Muller and wife Sara harbor refugees from Nazi Germany; Kurt kills the blackmailing Romanian count Teck de Brancovis.

entries.note: Southern lyric dramatist: A Streetcar Named Desire, The Glass Menagerie, Cat on a Hot Tin Roof, Suddenly, Last Summer, and The Night of the Iguana.

works.note: Williams play in which Blanche DuBois arrives at Stella and Stanley Kowalski's Elysian Fields apartment; "STELLA!", the paper lantern, and "the kindness of strangers" are giveaways.
Fading Southern belle of A Streetcar Named Desire; raped by brother-in-law Stanley Kowalski, she is led away insisting she has "always depended on the kindness of strangers."
Pregnant sister of Blanche DuBois in A Streetcar Named Desire; her name is famously bellowed by husband Stanley Kowalski after their poker-night fight.
Williams's "memory play": narrator Tom Wingfield, his mother Amanda, his fragile sister Laura with her glass unicorn, and the gentleman caller Jim O'Connor.
Painfully shy "Blue Roses" of The Glass Menagerie; her glass unicorn's broken horn seals the heartbreak of the gentleman caller Jim O'Connor's visit.

works.topic: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof
works.answerline: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof
works.note: Williams play set at Big Daddy Pollitt's birthday on a Mississippi Delta plantation; alcoholic Brick, his wife Maggie "the Cat," and the truth about Skipper drive the plot.
Cat on a Hot Tin Roof's central theme: Brick blames it for labeling his bond with Skipper as gay, and for Big Daddy's cancer denial.
Williams play set at Maxine Faulk's Costa Verde hotel in Mexico: defrocked Reverend Shannon, artist Hannah Jelkes, and poet grandfather Nonno; ends as Shannon unties the title reptile.
Williams's only Tony-winning play: widow Serafina delle Rose falls for banana-truck driver Alvaro after her husband Rosario's affair and death are revealed.
Williams play in which Mrs. Venable seeks a lobotomy for niece Catherine Holly to silence her account of cousin Sebastian Venable being devoured by children.

entries.note: Playwright of Death of a Salesman, The Crucible, All My Sons, and A View from the Bridge; his marriage to Marilyn Monroe and HUAC testimony appear as biographical clues.

works.note: Miller's tragedy of Willy Loman and his sons Biff and Happy; "attention must be paid," the rubber hose, and the Requiem's "We're free and clear" are stock clues.
Death of a Salesman's title character, an aging traveling salesman whose broken refrigerator and rubber hose in the basement foreshadow his suicide for insurance money.
Willy Loman's elder son in Death of a Salesman; discovering his father's affair with The Woman derails his University of Virginia football scholarship.
Miller's Salem witch-trial allegory of McCarthyism: John Proctor's cry "Because it is my name!", the accuser Abigail Williams, and Giles Corey pressed to death demanding "more weight."
The Crucible's protagonist, whose affair with Abigail Williams fuels the accusations; he tears up his false confession, crying "I have given you my soul; leave me my name!"
Enslaved woman in The Crucible who admits giving Betty chicken blood and accuses Goody Good and Goody Osborne, sparking the Salem witchcraft hysteria.
Miller play about Joe Keller, who shipped cracked cylinder heads that killed twenty-one pilots; his son Larry's suicide letter forces the recognition that "they were all my sons."
Miller play of Red Hook longshoreman Eddie Carbone, whose jealous obsession with niece Catherine leads him to inform on immigrants Marco and Rodolpho.
Miller's semi-autobiographical play narrated by lawyer Quentin, whose failed marriages (one echoing Marilyn Monroe) unfold before the silhouette of a Nazi camp tower.

entries.note: First Black woman to have a play on Broadway, with A Raisin in the Sun; The Sign in Sidney Brustein's Window and To Be Young, Gifted and Black also appear.

works.note: Hansberry's play about the Youngers' insurance money and move to white Clybourne Park: Walter Lee's liquor-store dream, Beneatha, Mama's plant, and Karl Lindner's buyout offer.
Aspiring doctor in A Raisin in the Sun who favors Nigerian suitor Joseph Asagai over the assimilated George Murchison; wears her hair natural.

works.topic: The Sign in Sidney Brustein's Window
works.answerline: The Sign in Sidney Brustein's Window
works.note: Hansberry's last play, set among Greenwich Village intellectuals: publisher Sidney Brustein backs populist candidate Wally O'Hara; completed by husband Robert Nemiroff after her death.

entries.note: Author of the ten-play Pittsburgh Cycle chronicling each decade of twentieth-century Black life: Fences, The Piano Lesson, Ma Rainey's Black Bottom, and Joe Turner's Come and Gone.

works.note: Wilson's Pulitzer winner about ex-Negro Leagues slugger Troy Maxson, who builds a fence, taunts Death, and blocks his son Cory's football scholarship; Gabriel opens the gates at the end.
Protagonist of Fences: ex-Negro Leagues slugger turned garbage collector who blocks son Cory's football scholarship and fathers Raynell with his mistress Alberta.
Wilson play in which Boy Willie wants to sell the piano carved with the family's history, which his sister Berniece refuses to touch; it ends with the exorcism of Sutter's ghost.
Wilson's only Pittsburgh Cycle play set outside Pittsburgh: at a Chicago recording session, trumpeter Levee stabs bandleader Toledo after Ma Rainey fires him.
Blues singer of Ma Rainey's Black Bottom who refuses to record without a Coca-Cola and fires the hot-tempered trumpeter Levee from her band.
Pittsburgh Cycle entry set in the 1910s: conjure man Bynum aids Herald Loomis, freed from Joe Turner's chain gang, in finding his lost wife.
Cycle's earliest chronological entry: 285-year-old "washer of souls" Aunt Ester sends Citizen Barlow to the City of Bones aboard a paper boat.
Recurring centuries-old healer of the Pittsburgh Cycle, living at 1839 Wylie Avenue; most prominent in Gem of the Ocean, guiding Citizen Barlow.
Name shared by two Pittsburgh Cycle characters: an ex-con who sells stolen refrigerators in King Hedley II, and Seven Guitars' killer of Floyd Barton.
August Wilson's ten-play series, one set per decade of twentieth-century Black life; Gem of the Ocean and Radio Golf bookend it, Aunt Ester recurs.
Common link built on August Wilson's ten-play cycle in the Hill District, plus Chabon's The Mysteries of Pittsburgh, Cather's "Paul's Case," and Annie Dillard's An American Childhood.

entries.note: Playwright bridging absurdism and domestic drama: Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?, The Zoo Story, A Delicate Balance, and The Goat, or Who Is Sylvia?

works.note: Albee's play of George and Martha's all-night "Fun and Games" with guests Nick and Honey, climaxing in the "killing" of George and Martha's imaginary son.
Albee's 1967 Pulitzer winner: Harry and Edna move in with Tobias and Agnes, fleeing a nameless terror, displacing daughter Julia from her room.
Albee play in which retired couple Nancy and Charlie, resting on a beach, meet the evolved lizard couple Leslie and Sarah and discuss evolution.
Albee's one-act on a Central Park bench: drifter Jerry tells the "story of Jerry and the dog," provokes publisher Peter, and impales himself on the knife he makes Peter hold.
Albee one-act in which Mommy and Daddy dump Grandma in the title sandbox as the calisthenics-performing Young Man, the Angel of Death, comes for her.
Albee play in which Pritzker-winning architect Martin confesses an affair with a goat named Sylvia; wife Stevie kills and drags the animal onstage in revenge.

entries.note: Playwright of clipped, profane "Mamet speak": Glengarry Glen Ross, American Buffalo, Oleanna, and Speed-the-Plow.

works.note: Mamet's Pulitzer winner about desperate Chicago real-estate salesmen — Shelly Levene, Ricky Roma, the stolen Glengarry leads, and the film's added "Always be closing" speech.
Mamet play set in a junk shop: Don and Teach plot to steal back a rare buffalo nickel, botching the burglary after distrusting young Bobby.
Two-hander in which student Carol accuses professor John of sexual harassment; the escalating conflict ends as John violently assaults her with a chair.

entries.note: Irish-American actor-manager and prolific melodramatist; wrote The Octoroon and the Irish drama The Colleen Bawn.
Boucicault melodrama set on the Terrebonne plantation; Zoe, one-eighth Black, cannot marry the man she loves under slavery-era law.
Tom Taylor's comedy of the bumbling Lord Dundreary; the play Lincoln was watching at Ford's Theatre when assassinated.
Turn-of-the-century producer-director famed for hyper-realistic staging; wrote the source plays behind Puccini's Madama Butterfly and The Girl of the Golden West.
Provincetown Players co-founder; wrote Trifles and won the Pulitzer for Alison's House, based on Emily Dickinson's life.
Glaspell's one-act in which farmwives Mrs. Peters and Mrs. Hale piece together Minnie Wright's motive for murder from kitchen "trifles."
Expressionist playwright of The Adding Machine; also won the Pulitzer for the tenement drama Street Scene, later a Kurt Weill opera.
Rice's expressionist play in which the clerk Mr. Zero murders his boss after being replaced by the title device.
Sophie Treadwell's expressionist play, loosely based on the Ruth Snyder case, of a stifled "Young Woman" who murders her husband.
Verse dramatist who drew on the Sacco and Vanzetti case for Winterset; also wrote Both Your Houses and, with Kurt Weill, Lost in the Stars.
Anderson's verse drama fictionalizing Sacco and Vanzetti: Mio Romagna seeks to clear his executed father, opposed by the murderer Trock Estrella.
Algonquin Round Table playwright and FDR speechwriter; three-time Drama Pulitzer winner for Idiot's Delight, Abe Lincoln in Illinois, and There Shall Be No Night.
Directed the all-Black "Voodoo" Macbeth and The Cradle Will Rock for the Federal Theatre Project before Citizen Kane and War of the Worlds.
Lee Strasberg's Group Theatre-rooted technique using "affective memory"; associated with Odets's Waiting for Lefty and the Actors Studio.

entries.topic: The Man Who Came to Dinner
entries.answerline: The Man Who Came to Dinner
entries.note: Kaufman and Hart comedy: acerbic critic Sheridan Whiteside, injured en route to dinner, torments his Ohio hosts as houseguest.
Wilder's play narrated by the Stage Manager in Grover's Corners, New Hampshire; Emily Webb marries George Gibbs, dies, and relives her twelfth birthday.

works.note: Narrates Grover's Corners, marries George and Emily, and answers "the saints and poets, maybe" to Emily's question.
Our Town's heroine, who marries George Gibbs, dies in childbirth, and relives her twelfth birthday from the grave.

entries.note: Wilder's fourth-wall-breaking allegory of the Antrobus family of Excelsior, New Jersey, surviving an ice age, flood, and war with maid Sabina.
"Playwright of the Midwest": Picnic, Bus Stop, and Come Back, Little Sheba; also wrote the screenplay for Splendor in the Grass.

works.note: Inge's Pulitzer winner: drifter Hal Carter disrupts a small Kansas town's Labor Day picnic, romancing Madge Owens.
Inge play snowbound at a Kansas diner: nightclub "chantoosie" Cherie fends off cowboy Bo Decker with sheriff Will Masters's help.

entries.note: Armenian-American writer who declined the Pulitzer for The Time of Your Life, his play set in a waterfront saloon.
Saroyan's Pulitzer-declined play set among barflies in Nick's Pacific Street Saloon in San Francisco.
Lawrence and Lee's fictionalized Scopes "Monkey" Trial: attorneys Henry Drummond and Matthew Harrison Brady clash over teaching evolution.
Amiri Baraka's play set on a New York subway car: the white Lula seduces then fatally stabs the Black passenger Clay.
Prolific Broadway comedy writer: The Odd Couple's Oscar and Felix, Barefoot in the Park, the autobiographical Brighton Beach trilogy, and the Pulitzer-winning Lost in Yonkers.

works.note: Simon's comedy pairing slovenly Oscar Madison with fussy, neurotic Felix Ungar as mismatched roommates.

entries.note: Playwright of Love! Valour! Compassion! and Master Class, on Maria Callas; also wrote librettos for Ragtime and Kiss of the Spider Woman.

works.note: McNally play following eight gay men through a summer of weekends at a country house upstate.

entries.note: Steppenwolf-affiliated playwright and actor; won the Pulitzer for August: Osage County.

works.note: Letts's Pulitzer winner: the pill-addicted matriarch Violet Weston and her daughters clash after patriarch Beverly's disappearance and death.

entries.note: Playwright of the two-part AIDS-crisis epic Angels in America; also adapted Angels for HBO and wrote Caroline, or Change.

works.topic: Angels in America: A Gay Fantasia on National Themes
works.answerline: Angels in America: A Gay Fantasia on National Themes
works.note: Prophet-despite-himself Prior Walter, closeted Joe Pitt, Roy Cohn, and an Angel crashing through the ceiling.
Disease driving Angels in America's plot, as Prior Walter's diagnosis and Roy Cohn's denial of his own.
Common-link keyed to the Angel who crashes through Prior Walter's ceiling, heralding him as a reluctant prophet in Angels in America.
Real-life McCarthy-era lawyer fictionalized in Angels in America, dying of AIDS while denying he has it.

entries.note: AIDS activist who founded ACT UP and Gay Men's Health Crisis; dramatized the epidemic's early years in The Normal Heart.
Kramer's autobiographical AIDS-crisis drama; Ned Weeks battles indifference while founding a gay health organization in early-1980s New York.
Won the Pulitzer for How I Learned to Drive, on Li'l Bit's abuse by Uncle Peck; also wrote Indecent, on Sholem Asch's God of Vengeance.
Comic playwright of the one-act collection All in the Timing, including "Sure Thing," whose bell resets a flirtation until it succeeds; also wrote Venus in Fur.
First Black woman to win the Drama Pulitzer, for Topdog/Underdog; also wrote The America Play and Father Comes Home from the Wars.

works.note: Parks's play of brothers named Lincoln and Booth; Lincoln works as a Lincoln-impersonator reenacting the assassination at an arcade.

entries.note: Off-Off-Broadway-rooted playwright of Buried Child, True West, and Fool for Love; also an actor in The Right Stuff.

works.note: Shepard's Pulitzer winner: a Midwestern farm family conceals the corpse of an infant buried in the backyard; grandson Vince returns home.

entries.note: Tony winner for M. Butterfly; also wrote Yellow Face and Chinglish, often exploring Asian-American identity and stereotype.

works.note: Hwang's riff on Puccini: French diplomat Gallimard's decades-long affair with opera singer Song Liling, revealed to be a man and spy.

entries.note: Pulitzer-winning playwright of Disgraced; also wrote the Wall Street drama Junk and the autobiographical novel Homeland Elegies.

works.note: Akhtar's play in which lawyer Amir Kapoor's dinner party unravels after he admits complicated feelings about 9/11 and Islam.

entries.note: Playwright of Six Degrees of Separation and The House of Blue Leaves, often centered on con artistry and thwarted ambition.
Memphis-set playwright; won the Pulitzer for The Hot Wing King and wrote The Mountaintop, imagining MLK's last night with the maid Camae.
Wrote the book for In the Heights; won the Pulitzer for Water by the Spoonful, part of her Iraq War veteran Elliot Ortiz trilogy.
Shanley's play in which Sister Aloysius accuses Father Flynn of abusing a student, with Sister James caught between them.
Margaret Edson's Pulitzer winner: John Donne scholar Vivian Bearing endures ovarian-cancer treatment, reciting "Death, be not proud" as she dies.
Eve Ensler's episodic play built from interviews about women's bodies, sexuality, and experiences.
Wrote the "choreopoem" for colored girls who have considered suicide/when the rainbow is enuf, blending poetry, dance, and music.
Nilo Cruz's Pulitzer winner: a lector reads Anna Karenina aloud to Cuban cigar rollers in 1920s Ybor City, Tampa.
Bruce Norris's Pulitzer/Tony-winning Raisin in the Sun spin-off: white couple Bev and Russ sell their house despite Karl Lindner's objections.
Tectonic Theater Project's docudrama, built from interviews after the murder of Matthew Shepard in Laramie, Wyoming.
First woman to win two Drama Pulitzers: Ruined, set in a bar-brothel in wartime Congo, and Sweat, on factory workers in Reading, Pennsylvania.
Common-link state: setting of August Wilson's Pittsburgh Cycle, Lynn Nottage's Sweat, and Quiara Alegria Hudes's Water by the Spoonful.
Common-link surname: August Wilson's Pittsburgh Cycle plays and Lanford Wilson's Talley's Folly and Fifth of July.
Common-link prize: won by Hudes's Water by the Spoonful, Akhtar's Disgraced, four times by O'Neill, and three times by Albee.
Common-link title word: O'Neill's Desire Under the Elms, Williams's A Streetcar Named Desire, and Glaspell's Suppressed Desires.

sections.name: Postwar Poetry and the Beats
sections.blurb: Postwar American verse fractures into confessional self-exposure, Beat countercultural sprawl, and New York School wit, before opening into today's laureates and performance poetry.

entries.note: Confessional poet of Ariel — "Daddy," "Lady Lazarus" ("I eat men like air") — and The Colossus; The Bell Jar and her marriage to Ted Hughes appear constantly.

works.note: Plath's autobiographical novel of Esther Greenwood's New York magazine internship, breakdown, and shock treatment; first published under the pseudonym Victoria Lucas.
Narrator of The Bell Jar; a magazine intern under Jay Cee who undergoes electroconvulsive therapy after a breakdown and suicide attempt.
Plath's posthumous collection, published by Hughes; its title poem is a fierce dawn horse ride "into the red eye, the cauldron of morning."
Plath poem addressing her dead father as a "man in black with a Meinkampf look"; opens "You do not do, you do not do."
Otto Plath, recalled as a "bag full of God" and "panzer-man" in "Daddy," and mourned in "Electra on Azalea Path" and "Little Fugue."
Plath poem of serial near-death and rebirth: "Out of the ash / I rise with my red hair / And I eat men like air."
English poet and Plath's husband; his Birthday Letters answered her confessional poems decades later, and his Crow followed her 1963 suicide.

entries.note: Meticulous poet of "One Art" ("the art of losing isn't hard to master"), "The Fish," "In the Waiting Room," and "The Moose"; lived for years in Brazil with Lota de Macedo Soares.

works.note: Villanelle listing escalating losses, from door keys to "a continent"; written after Lota de Macedo Soares's suicide, ending "(Write it!)".
Poem of the title animal fleeing Brazilian fire-balloons at a saint's-day festival; dedicated to Robert Lowell, who answered it with "Skunk Hour."
Poem of an old, battle-scarred fish held boatside and released once the speaker sees "rainbow, rainbow, rainbow" in the oil-slicked water; Marianne Moore wrote a same-titled poem.

entries.note: Founder of confessional poetry with Life Studies; "Skunk Hour," "For the Union Dead," and "Memories of West Street and Lepke," from his conscientious-objector jail term, recur.

works.note: Lowell poem set on Nautilus Island, ending as a skunk "jabs her wedge-head in a cup / of sour cream"; dedicated to Elizabeth Bishop, answering her "The Armadillo."
Poem on the Robert Gould Shaw memorial, "a fishbone in the city's throat," set against a Boston parking garage under construction.
Lowell's confession-launching 1959 collection; includes the prose memoir "91 Revere Street" and poems on his mental illness, ending with "Skunk Hour."

entries.note: Confessional poet who began writing in therapy: To Bedlam and Part Way Back, the Pulitzer-winning Live or Die, and the fairy-tale revisions of Transformations.
Confessional poet of The Dream Songs, starring Henry and the minstrel-voiced "Mr. Bones," and Homage to Mistress Bradstreet; he jumped from a Minneapolis bridge.

works.note: Berryman's 385-poem sequence for alter ego Henry and his blackface interlocutor "Mr. Bones"; twelve elegies were dedicated to Delmore Schwartz.

entries.note: Movement label for intensely autobiographical postwar verse; Lowell's Life Studies, Plath, Sexton, Berryman, and W. D. Snodgrass's Heart's Needle are the canonical clues.
New York poet-critic whose story "In Dreams Begin Responsibilities" and poem "In the Naked Bed, In Plato's Cave" made him the model for Saul Bellow's Humboldt's Gift.

works.note: Schwartz's title story: a man watches a film of his parents' courtship and disastrous marriage, shouting warnings at the screen.

entries.note: Poet-critic best known for the five-line "The Death of the Ball Turret Gunner," which ends "they washed me out of the turret with a hose"; also wrote Pictures from an Institution.

works.topic: "The Death of the Ball Turret Gunner"
works.answerline: "The Death of the Ball Turret Gunner"
works.note: Five-line WWII poem: "From my mother's sleep I fell into the State"; ends with the gunner "washed...out of the turret with a hose."

entries.note: Michigan-born poet of "My Papa's Waltz," the whiskey-drunk father who "beat time" on the speaker's head; also wrote "The Waking" and the greenhouse poem "Root Cellar."
New York School poet whose Self-Portrait in a Convex Mirror, on Parmigianino's painting, swept the Pulitzer, National Book Award, and NBCC; famously difficult and prolific.

works.note: Ashbery's title poem on Parmigianino's self-portrait, "the right hand / Bigger than the head, thrust at the viewer"; won a rare Pulitzer/NBA/NBCC sweep.

entries.note: New York School poet and MoMA curator; Lunch Poems pieces like "The Day Lady Died," on Billie Holiday's death, and "Why I Am Not a Painter" recur.

works.note: O'Hara's collection of quick, city-life poems written on lunch breaks; includes "The Day Lady Died" and "Lana Turner has collapsed!"

entries.note: New York School poet of "Fresh Air," mocking academic taste, and "Why I Am Not a Painter" 's foil; parodied William Carlos Williams's "This Is Just to Say."
James Merrill's Ouija-board epic in all capitals, voiced by spirits Ephraim and Mirabell to "DJ" and "JM"; set at his Stonington, Connecticut house.
1950s counterculture circle of Ginsberg, Kerouac, Burroughs, and Neal Cassady, centered on City Lights bookstore and the Six Gallery reading; Kerouac coined the name.
Beat poet of "Howl," "America," and "Kaddish," his elegy for his mother Naomi; "A Supermarket in California" imagines meeting Whitman among the groceries.

works.note: Ginsberg's Beat epic beginning "I saw the best minds of my generation destroyed by madness"; addresses Carl Solomon ("I'm with you in Rockland"), rails against Moloch, and survived a 1957 obscenity trial.
Mental institution in "Howl" 's third section, repeatedly addressed to Carl Solomon: "I'm with you in Rockland where you're madder than I am."
Ginsberg's elegy for his mother Naomi, opening "Strange now to think of you, gone without corsets & eyes"; titled for the Jewish mourning prayer.

entries.note: Beat novelist of On the Road, typed on a continuous scroll, with Sal Paradise and Dean Moriarty; The Dharma Bums and Big Sur also appear.

works.note: Novel of cross-country trips with Dean Moriarty (based on Neal Cassady); Sal Paradise narrates, encountering Old Bull Lee and jazz pianist George Shearing.
Narrator of On the Road; steals a copy of Le Grand Meaulnes, picks cotton with Terry, and criss-crosses the U.S. with Dean Moriarty.

entries.note: Beat author of the cut-up novel Naked Lunch, plus Junky and Queer; he killed his wife Joan Vollmer in a drunken "William Tell" act in Mexico City.

works.note: Cut-up novel of addict Bill Lee wandering Interzone; Dr. Benway runs a Reconditioning Center, and denizens eat the "Black Meat" of giant centipedes.

entries.note: Beat poet and Zen practitioner who read "A Berry Feast" at the Six Gallery; wrote the environmentalist collection Turtle Island and inspired Japhy Ryder in Kerouac's The Dharma Bums.

works.note: Snyder's Pulitzer-winning collection titled with a Native American name for North America; includes "Smokey the Bear Sutra" and "The Bath."

entries.note: Beat-era poet who co-founded San Francisco's City Lights bookstore and published Ginsberg's "Howl," triggering an obscenity trial; wrote A Coney Island of the Mind.

works.topic: A Coney Island of the Mind
works.answerline: A Coney Island of the Mind
works.note: Ferlinghetti's collection opening "In Goya's greatest scenes we seem to see"; includes "Junkman's Obbligato" and the tightrope-walking "Constantly Risking Absurdity."

entries.note: Countercultural novelist-poet of the post-apocalyptic commune iDEATH in In Watermelon Sugar; Trout Fishing in America gives its title to a person, a ballet, and a book.

works.note: Brautigan's genre-blurring book whose title names a person, a hotel, and a ballet across its loosely linked vignettes.

entries.note: San Francisco bookstore and press co-founded by Lawrence Ferlinghetti; published Ginsberg's Howl and Other Poems, sparking his 1957 obscenity trial.
Avant-garde group centered at the North Carolina college, including Charles Olson, Robert Creeley, Robert Duncan, and Denise Levertov; Olson's "Projective Verse" was its manifesto.
Religion adopted by many Beat writers, including Gary Snyder (Japhy Ryder's model) and Ginsberg ("Sunflower Sutra," "Wichita Vortex Sutra"); Kerouac's The Dharma Bums follows its seekers.
Skid-row poet-novelist of Los Angeles and his alter ego Henry Chinaski: Post Office, Ham on Rye, Factotum, and thousands of plainspoken poems.
Formalist poet of "Love Calls Us to the Things of This World," where laundry-line angels fill "the morning air"; mentored the New Formalist poets.
Pulitzer-winning poet of The Hard Hours; Holocaust poems "More Light! More Light!" and "The Book of Yolek" draw on his WWII service in Germany.
Poet of colon-linked free verse; Garbage, inspired by a Florida landfill, won the National Book Award, and "Corsons Inlet" surveys a New Jersey beach walk.
Georgian poet-novelist of Deliverance, in which Ed Gentry survives a mountain-men attack; also wrote "Buckdancer's Choice" and read at Jimmy Carter's inauguration.
Poet of "A Blessing," where two Indian ponies' "eyes / Darken with kindness," and "Lying in a Hammock..." ending "I have wasted my life."
Berkeley poet and former U.S. Poet Laureate who translated neighbor Czeslaw Milosz; "Meditation at Lagunitas" repeats "blackberry, blackberry, blackberry."
Serbian-American Pulitzer winner of The World Doesn't End; prose poems and "Watermelons" draw on his WWII Eastern European childhood, per "Eyes Fastened with Pins."
English-born American poet of The Man with Night Sweats, elegizing friends lost to AIDS; "On the Move" depicts a leather-clad motorcycle gang.
Poet-activist of The Book of the Dead, on the Hawk's Nest mining disaster; "To Be a Jew in the Twentieth Century" calls it a "gift" one cannot refuse.
Feminist poet of "Diving into the Wreck," "Aunt Jennifer's Tigers," and Snapshots of a Daughter-in-Law; she refused the National Medal of Arts in 1997.
Muscogee (Creek) poet and the first Native American U.S. Poet Laureate; "She Had Some Horses," An American Sunrise, and "Perhaps the World Ends Here" are common clues.
Nobel- and Pulitzer-winning poet of The Wild Iris, whose flowers "hate" like the speaker; retold the Odyssey in Meadowlands and the Iliad in The Triumph of Achilles.
Former U.S. Poet Laureate of Thomas and Beulah; Sonata Mulattica follows biracial violinist George Bridgetower, and her Penguin anthology drew fire from Helen Vendler.
Mississippi-born former U.S. Poet Laureate of Native Guard, on a Black Civil War regiment and her mother's death; also wrote the Bellocq-inspired Bellocq's Ophelia.
African-American Vietnam veteran poet of Dien Cai Dau and "Facing It," on the Vietnam Veterans Memorial; won the Pulitzer for Neon Vernacular.
Current U.S. Poet Laureate of Mexican-American descent; wrote "How to Triumph Like a Girl," "A New National Anthem," and the collections Bright Dead Things and The Carrying.
Bestselling, accessible U.S. Poet Laureate of Picnic, Lightning and The Trouble with Poetry; wrote "The Best Cigarette" and "Dancing toward Bethlehem."
Nature poet of "Wild Geese" ("You do not have to be good") and "The Summer Day"; helped organize Edna St. Vincent Millay's papers at Steepletop.
Children's poet-illustrator of The Giving Tree and Where the Sidewalk Ends; also wrote Johnny Cash's "A Boy Named Sue" and the screenplay for Things Change.
Competitive spoken-word format invented by Chicago construction worker Marc Smith and popularized at the Nuyorican Poets Cafe; poems are judged by audience scorecards.
Broader performance genre of poets reading aloud to audience approval, from Louise Bennett-Coverley's dialect verse to Gil Scott-Heron; slam poetry is its competitive, judged form.

sections.name: Postwar Fiction (1945-1980)
sections.blurb: Fiction's postwar boom: suburban short stories give way to Jewish-American social novels, postmodern maximalism, and Vietnam-era New Journalism blurring fact and fiction.

entries.note: Reclusive author of The Catcher in the Rye and the Glass family stories — Franny and Zooey and "A Perfect Day for Bananafish," ending in Seymour's suicide.

works.note: Opening story of Nine Stories: Seymour Glass talks to young Sybil Carpenter about bananafish on a Florida beach, then shoots himself.
Nine Stories entry in which Eloise Wengler, drinking with old college friend Mary Jane, mourns her wartime love Walt while daughter Ramona plays.
Collection opening with "A Perfect Day for Bananafish" and including "For Esme — with Love and Squalor" and "Uncle Wiggily in Connecticut."
Eldest, spiritually precocious Glass sibling who describes the title creatures to Sybil Carpenter on the beach before shooting himself in "Bananafish."
Fictional fish that gorge on bananas in underwater holes until too fat to escape, described by Seymour Glass to Sybil Carpenter.
Young English girl who befriends a traumatized American soldier before D-Day in "For Esme — with Love and Squalor."
Youngest Glass sibling whose breakdown over The Way of a Pilgrim, recited to boyfriend Lane Coutell, is chronicled in Franny and Zooey.
Alienated seventeen-year-old narrator of The Catcher in the Rye, disgusted by "phonies" during his two-day wander through New York City.

entries.note: "Chekhov of the suburbs": stories like "The Swimmer," "The Enormous Radio," and "The Five-Forty-Eight," plus the Wapshot novels and prison novel Falconer.

works.note: Neddy Merrill swims home across the county through the "Lucinda River" of backyard pools, arriving at an empty, locked house as the seasons inexplicably turn.
Jim and Irene Westcott's new radio lets them eavesdrop on neighbors' secrets, curdling their own "satisfactory" marriage.
Cheever's fictional Westchester suburb, setting of "The Country Husband," "The Five-Forty-Eight," and "The Housebreaker of Shady Hill."

entries.note: Nobel-winning novelist of Jewish Chicago: The Adventures of Augie March, Herzog with its unsent letters, Humboldt's Gift, Seize the Day, and Henderson the Rain King.

works.note: Divorced academic Moses Herzog writes unsent letters to Nietzsche, Eisenhower, and the dead after his wife Madeleine leaves him for his friend Valentine Gersbach.
Picaresque bildungsroman opening "I am an American, Chicago born"; the title character survives a torpedoed ship and trains an eagle in Mexico.
Bellow's picaresque title character, raised by Grandma Lausch and shaped by Einhorn; cites Heraclitus while declaring himself "an American, Chicago born."
Millionaire pig farmer who travels to Africa, blows up a cistern of frogs, and earns the title "Rain King" from the Wariri tribe.
Novel following Eugene Henderson's African journey with guide Romilayu and King Dahfu, moving a statue of the goddess Mummah.

entries.note: Chronicler of Jewish Newark and alter ego Nathan Zuckerman: Portnoy's Complaint, American Pastoral, The Human Stain, Goodbye, Columbus, and The Plot Against America.

works.note: Roth's 1969 succes de scandale: Alexander Portnoy's unbroken monologue to psychoanalyst Dr. Spielvogel about his mother Sophie and his compulsions; the liver scene is infamous.
Pulitzer-winning novel in which Nathan Zuckerman recounts Seymour "Swede" Levov's shattered idyll after daughter Merry bombs a post office over Vietnam.
Classics professor Coleman Silk, fired over the word "spooks," is revealed by narrator Nathan Zuckerman to be a black man passing as white.
Title novella and story collection: Neil Klugman's summer romance with wealthy Brenda Patimkin ends after her parents discover her diaphragm.
Roth's recurring novelist narrator and alter ego, appearing from The Ghost Writer through American Pastoral, The Human Stain, and Exit Ghost.

entries.note: Reclusive postmodernist: Gravity's Rainbow, The Crying of Lot 49, V., Mason & Dixon, and Inherent Vice; entropy and paranoia are his signature clue-words.

works.note: Pynchon's V-2 rocket epic opening "A screaming comes across the sky": Tyrone Slothrop's conditioned map of London, Rocket 00000, and the postwar Zone.
Pynchon novella in which Oedipa Maas, executing Pierce Inverarity's estate, uncovers the Trystero and the muted post horn of the underground mail system W.A.S.T.E.
2013 novel following fraud investigator Maxine Tarnow's probe of the security firm hashslingrz, set against the backdrop of 9/11.
Callisto nurses a dying bird in a hermetically sealed room while Meatball Mulligan's lease-breaking party rages downstairs; applies thermodynamics to culture and communication.
Sprawling 2006 novel following the airship crew the Chums of Chance across the decades before World War I.
Novel narrated by Reverend Wicks Cherrycoke, recounting surveyors Mason and Dixon's line with cameos by Franklin and Washington.
Weapons central to Gravity's Rainbow's Tyrone Slothrop plot, from "The White Visitation" research group to Rocket 00000; Pynchon once wrote for their manufacturer.
Debut novel in which Herbert Stencil searches for the mysterious title figure while Benny Profane drifts through the Whole Sick Crew.

entries.note: Satirist blending science fiction and dark humor: Billy Pilgrim comes "unstuck in time," recurring author-avatar Kilgore Trout, and the alien Tralfamadorians.

works.note: Vonnegut novel of ice-nine, which freezes the world's water; narrator John/Jonah, the invented religion of Bokononism, and the island of San Lorenzo are the clues.

works.answerline: Slaughterhouse-Five, or The Children's Crusade: A Duty-Dance with Death
works.note: Billy Pilgrim, "unstuck in time," survives the Dresden firebombing and is exhibited by Tralfamadorian aliens with Montana Wildhack.
Vonnegut's recurring hack science-fiction author, whose fan Eliot Rosewater and book Now It Can Be Told drive other characters' plots.
Optometrist and Dresden survivor who comes "unstuck in time" and is abducted by the toilet-plunger-shaped Tralfamadorians.
Story collection including "Harrison Bergeron" and the title story, in which "Nothinghead" Billy the Poet menaces an Ethical Suicide Parlor hostess.
"Autobiography" of Abstract Expressionist painter Rabo Karabekian, who guards his barn mural "Now It's the Women's Turn" from houseguest Circe Berman.

works.answerline: Breakfast of Champions, or Goodbye Blue Monday
works.note: Hack author Kilgore Trout's writing convinces car dealer Dwayne Hoover, in Midland City, that he alone has free will.
Philanthropist millionaire and devoted Kilgore Trout fan who recurs across Vonnegut's novels, including his own God Bless You, Mr. Rosewater.
Recurring Vonnegut surname: hack author Kilgore, his son Leon who narrates Galapagos, and other family across Vonnegut's fictional universe.

entries.note: New Journalism brawler: The Naked and the Dead, the Pulitzer-winning The Armies of the Night, and The Executioner's Song, about Gary Gilmore's execution.

works.topic: "The White Negro: Superficial Reflections on the Hipster"
works.answerline: "The White Negro: Superficial Reflections on the Hipster"
works.note: Essay casting the "hipster" as existentialist outsider fusing jazz, danger, and the language of Hip; collected in Advertisements for Myself.
Pulitzer-winning "true life novel" of Gary Gilmore, executed by firing squad in Utah after demanding his own death sentence be carried out.

entries.note: White-suited New Journalist: The Electric Kool-Aid Acid Test, The Right Stuff, and the novel The Bonfire of the Vanities with Sherman McCoy, self-styled "Master of the Universe."

works.note: Bond trader Sherman McCoy's hit-and-run of Henry Lamb in the Bronx, covered by journalist Peter Fallow, destroys his Wall Street status.
Campus novel following a scholarship student's disillusionment at fictional Dupont University amid Saint Ray fraternity antics and "Fuck Patois."

entries.note: Essayist of California unraveling: Slouching Towards Bethlehem, The White Album, the novel Play It as It Lays, and the memoir The Year of Magical Thinking.

works.note: Essay collection whose title essay recalls a Doors recording session and Manson witness Linda Kasabian; opens "We tell ourselves stories in order to live."
Essay collection on 1960s California unraveling; title essay profiles Haight-Ashbury runaways Jeff and Debbie as "the center was not holding."
Memoir of grief after husband John Gregory Dunne's sudden death; Didion keeps his shoes, unable to shake the belief he'll return.

entries.note: Author of Holden Caulfield's The Catcher in the Rye; Nine Stories includes "A Perfect Day for Bananafish" and recluse Seymour Glass.
Lyricist of suburban adultery: four Rabbit Angstrom novels, "A&P" narrated by checkout boy Sammy, and The Witches of Eastwick.

works.note: Angstrom, Updike's everyman protagonist across four novels (Run, Redux, Is Rich, At Rest) tracking postwar suburban decline.

entries.note: Satirist of Slaughterhouse-Five's Billy Pilgrim, Cat's Cradle, and Breakfast of Champions; recurring alter-ego novelist Kilgore Trout.
Narratives of Empire historical novelist (Burr); Myra Breckinridge's transitioning title character; sharp political essayist, sparred with Buckley.
Postmodern metafictionist of The Sot-Weed Factor and Giles Goat-Boy; Lost in the Funhouse's "Frame-Tale" opens with a Mobius strip.

works.note: Novel titled for Ebenezer Cooke's satirical poem on colonial Maryland; Barth's fictionalized Cooke is its "Poet Laureate" protagonist.
Satire in which a boy raised among goats becomes Grand Tutor of New Tammany College, trained by supercomputer WESCAC.

entries.note: Metafictional post-1950s movement of unreliable narrators and intertextuality; Barth's The Sot-Weed Factor and Lost in the Funhouse are key texts.
Russian-American novelist and lepidopterist who switched to English after emigrating; translated Eugene Onegin, taught at Cornell.

works.note: Novel narrated by Humbert Humbert ("light of my life, fire of my loins") obsessing over Dolores Haze; rival Clare Quilty, motel odyssey.
Novel structured as Charles Kinbote's mad commentary on John Shade's 999-line poem; assassin Jakob Gradus, exiled kingdom of Zembla.

entries.note: Postmodern short-story writer and metafictionist; his first novel retells a classic fairy tale.

works.note: Fragmented retelling in which Snow White lives with seven men who wash windows and bathe her; Jane loves Hogo de Bergerac.

entries.note: Reclusive postmodernist of J R and Agape Agape; The Recognitions took Jonathan Franzen a week and a half to read.
William Gaddis's dense debut novel of art forger Wyatt Gwyon, who fakes Flemish Old Masters under critic Basil Valentine's eye.
Countercultural novelist and Merry Prankster who drove the bus Furthur; The Electric Kool-Aid Acid Test chronicles his LSD circle.

works.topic: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
works.answerline: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
works.note: Asylum novel narrated by Chief Bromden: gambler Randle McMurphy battles Nurse Ratched, is lobotomized, and is smothered by the Chief.
Tyrannical head nurse of the asylum ward in One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest; McMurphy's chief antagonist.

entries.note: Historical novelist blending fiction with real figures; also wrote The Book of Daniel and Billy Bathgate.

works.note: Novel weaving Coalhouse Walker Jr.'s Model T revenge plot with historical figures — Houdini, Emma Goldman, J. P. Morgan — in 1900s New York.

entries.note: Satirist of Catch-22; also wrote Something Happened, a bleak corporate-life novel, and God Knows.

works.note: WWII satire of bombardier Yossarian on Pianosa; the title's circular logic on flying missions, Milo Minderbinder's syndicate, Snowden's death.
Catch-22's bombardier protagonist, desperate to be grounded as required missions keep rising; convinced everyone is trying to kill him.
Catch-22's mess officer who builds a black-market syndicate, even contracting to bomb his own airfield for profit.

entries.note: Jewish-American novelist of The Fixer and The Assistant; also wrote the baseball novel The Natural.

works.note: Novel of mysterious slugger Roy Hobbs, shot by Harriet Bird, chasing redemption for the Knights; loosely filmed with Robert Redford.

entries.note: Novelist of Orthodox and Hasidic Jewish life; also wrote My Name Is Asher Lev, of a painter's Brooklyn Crucifixion.
Novel of Orthodox Reuven Malter and Hasidic Danny Saunders, friends after a baseball injury; fathers Reb Saunders and David Malter.
Jewish-American author of "The Shawl," where baby Magda is killed at a camp; also wrote The Puttermesser Papers.
Laguna Pueblo author of Ceremony, in which WWII veteran Tayo is healed by medicine man Betonie; also wrote Almanac of the Dead.
Kiowa author of The Way to Rainy Mountain; his House Made of Dawn won the 1969 Pulitzer Prize.
N. Scott Momaday's Pulitzer-winning 1969 novel of Abel, a Kiowa veteran; ends in a ritual run honoring his grandfather Francisco.
Chronicler of suburban despair; Revolutionary Road's Frank and April Wheeler, The Easter Parade's Grimes sisters.
Novelist-critic of The Group, tracking eight 1933 Vassar graduates; wife of Edmund Wilson, feuded publicly with Lillian Hellman.
Chicago novelist of the lower depths: morphine addict Frankie Machine in The Man with the Golden Arm; Simone de Beauvoir's lover.
Blacklisted Hollywood Ten screenwriter; Johnny Got His Gun's Joe Bonham loses his limbs and face fighting in WWI.
Participatory journalist and Paris Review founding editor; Paper Lion recounts training with the Detroit Lions; wrote the Sidd Finch hoax.
Essayist-critic of "Notes on 'Camp,'" Against Interpretation, On Photography, and Illness as Metaphor; her novel The Volcano Lover also appears.
1960s-70s nonfiction movement blending reportage with subjective, novelistic technique; associated with Tom Wolfe, Joan Didion, Norman Mailer's convention pieces.

entries.topic: Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas
entries.answerline: Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas
entries.note: Hunter S. Thompson's gonzo novel of journalist Raoul Duke and Samoan attorney Dr. Gonzo's drug-fueled Vegas road trip.
New Yorker essayist and children's author of Stuart Little and Charlotte's Web; revised Strunk's The Elements of Style.
Common-link surname for married writers Paul (The Sheltering Sky, "A Distant Episode") and Jane Bowles (Two Serious Ladies); expatriates in Tangier.
Paul Bowles's novel of Port and Kit Moresby's crumbling marriage in North Africa; Port dies of typhoid, Kit is captured.
Richard Brautigan's novella of the commune iDEATH, built from the title substance; inBOIL's gang haunts the Forgotten Works.
Folk singer-novelist of Been Down So Long It Looks Like Up to Me's Gnossos Pappadopoulos; died young in a motorcycle crash.
Stanford creative-writing founder; The Big Rock Candy Mountain's Bo and Elsa Mason; wrote of explorer John Wesley Powell.
Pulitzer novel of wheelchair-bound historian Lyman Ward chronicling grandparents Susan and Oliver Ward's frontier marriage in mining towns.
Novelist of Stoner, a quiet campus-life tragedy; also wrote Butcher's Crossing and the epistolary Augustus.

sections.name: Contemporary Fiction (1980-present)
sections.blurb: American fiction since 1980 ranges from postmodern maximalism and Southern/Western violence through immigrant, Native, and autofictional voices reshaping the contemporary novel and short story.

entries.note: Postmodern maximalist of Infinite Jest, set partly at Enfield Tennis Academy; also wrote the essay "A Supposedly Fun Thing I'll Never Do Again" and the unfinished The Pale King.

works.note: Wallace's doorstop novel of the Incandenza family, wheelchair-bound Quebecois separatists, and a lethally entertaining "samizdat" film cartridge.

entries.note: Minimalist "dirty realist" story writer, heavily edited by Gordon Lish: "Cathedral," "Where I'm Calling From," and "What We Talk About When We Talk About Love."

works.topic: "What We Talk About When We Talk About Love"
works.answerline: "What We Talk About When We Talk About Love"
works.note: Title story: cardiologist Mel and Terri, plus Nick and Laura, drink gin and argue about love, recalling an old couple hurt in a crash.

variants.answerline: What We Talk About When We Talk About Love

works.note: A blind man, Robert, guides the narrator's hand to draw a cathedral together on paper while high.
Set at a drying-out facility; an alcoholic narrator listens to fellow patient J.P.'s life story.

entries.note: Novelist of violent Souths and Southwests: Blood Meridian with Judge Holden, the Border Trilogy beginning with All the Pretty Horses, No Country for Old Men, and The Road.

works.note: Post-apocalyptic Pulitzer winner following an unnamed father and son "carrying the fire" toward the coast, pushing a shopping cart through the ash.
Border Trilogy opener: John Grady Cole rides into Mexico, falls for ranch owner's daughter Alejandra, and is imprisoned.
Hairless, giant, erudite member of Glanton's scalp-hunting gang; declares "war is god" and shoots the ex-priest Tobin.
Llewelyn Moss stumbles on a drug deal's cash, pursued by the coin-flipping killer Anton Chigurh; Sheriff Bell narrates.
Ultraviolent Western following "the kid" through Glanton's scalp-hunting gang and its philosophizing judge across the Texas-Mexico borderlands.

entries.note: Postmodernist of White Noise, Underworld — which opens at the "Shot Heard 'Round the World" baseball game — Libra, and Mao II.

works.note: National Book Award-winning satire of Hitler-studies professor Jack Gladney, the Airborne Toxic Event, and the fear-of-death drug Dylar.
Waste-management executive Nick Shay, haunted by wife Marian's affair, anchors a Cold War epic opening with Cotter Martin catching a home-run ball.

entries.note: Chronicler of Bengali American life: the Pulitzer-winning Interpreter of Maladies, The Namesake with Gogol Ganguli, and later books written in Italian.
Hyper-prolific novelist; "Where Are You Going, Where Have You Been?" with Arnold Friend, the National Book Award winner them, and Blonde are the usual clues.

works.topic: "Where Are You Going, Where Have You Been?"
works.answerline: "Where Are You Going, Where Have You Been?"
works.note: Teenager Connie, alone at home, is menaced by the sinister, possibly supernatural stranger Arnold Friend.

entries.note: Pulitzer winner for The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier & Clay, about Golden Age comic-book creators; The Yiddish Policemen's Union and The Mysteries of Pittsburgh also come up.

works.topic: The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier and Clay
works.answerline: The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier and Clay
works.note: Joe Kavalier escapes Prague for New York, teams with cousin Sam Clay to create the superhero comic The Escapist.
Alternate history set in a Jewish settlement in Sitka, Alaska; detective Meyer Landsman investigates a murder amid looming resettlement.

entries.note: Novelist of Midwestern family sagas: The Corrections, about the Lambert family, and Freedom; his Oprah's Book Club snub is a recurring clue.

works.note: Novel of Patty and Walter Berglund's marriage and environmentalism; Patty gets a basketball scholarship to Minnesota.
Essay contrasting "Status" and "Contract" models of fiction while attacking William Gaddis's hard-to-finish novel J R.

entries.note: Spokane/Coeur d'Alene writer of The Lone Ranger and Tonto Fistfight in Heaven, the basis for the film Smoke Signals, and The Absolutely True Diary of a Part-Time Indian.
Satirical story writer of Tenth of December and CivilWarLand in Bad Decline; won the Booker Prize for Lincoln in the Bardo, narrated by graveyard ghosts.
Canadian short story writer and 2013 Nobel laureate; "The Bear Came Over the Mountain," Lives of Girls and Women, and Dance of the Happy Shades recur as clues.
Chinese American novelist best known for The Joy Luck Club; her fiction often traces immigrant mothers and American-born daughters across generations.

works.note: Four Chinese immigrant mothers and their American-born daughters trade stories over mahjong; Jing-mei "June" Woo travels to China to meet her half-sisters.

entries.note: Antiguan American writer known for the prose poem "Girl" and the novel Annie John, drawing on a mother-daughter relationship in the Caribbean.

works.note: A mother's stream of instructions to her daughter, from washing clothes to "the kind of woman the baker won't let near the bread."

entries.note: Novelist of The World According to Garp, The Cider House Rules, and A Prayer for Owen Meany, often set in New England.

works.note: T.S. Garp, son of feminist Jenny Fields, becomes a writer; the "Under Toad" and the Ellen Jamesians recur.
Orphan Homer Wells is trained in abortion medicine by Dr. Larch at a Maine orphanage, then works at an apple orchard.
Tiny-voiced Owen believes he is God's instrument and dies saving Vietnamese children; "keep passing the open windows" recurs.

entries.note: Novelist of The Poisonwood Bible, about the missionary Price family in the Congo, and the Pulitzer-winning Demon Copperhead, a Dickens-inspired Appalachian retelling.
Novelist of A Visit from the Goon Squad, a Pulitzer-winning story cycle told partly through a slideshow chapter, following music-industry figures across decades.

works.topic: A Visit From the Goon Squad
works.answerline: A Visit From the Goon Squad
works.note: Interlinked chapters follow kleptomaniac Sasha and record executive Bennie Salazar across decades; one chapter is a PowerPoint slideshow.

entries.note: Novelist of Everything Is Illuminated and Extremely Loud and Incredibly Close; also writes food-politics nonfiction like Eating Animals.

works.note: Nine-year-old Oskar Schell searches New York for a lock matching a key left by his father, who died on 9/11.

entries.note: Ojibwe novelist of Love Medicine and The Round House; her 2020 novel The Night Watchman draws on her grandfather's fight against Native termination policy.

works.note: Story cycle opening with June Kashpaw's death walking home in a snowstorm, following intertwined Ojibwe families on a North Dakota reservation.

entries.note: Common-link surname spanning sisters Louise, the celebrated novelist, and Heid, an Ojibwe poet; both are tied to Minneapolis's Birchbark Books.
Canadian novelist of The Handmaid's Tale and the speculative Oryx and Crake, often clued for dystopian and environmental themes.

works.note: Post-apocalyptic novel following Snowman/Jimmy, his genius friend Crake, and the genetically engineered Crakers after a bioengineered plague.

entries.note: Chicana writer of The House on Mango Street, a vignette novel narrated by young Esperanza Cordero in a Chicago barrio.

works.note: Vignette novel narrated by Esperanza Cordero, a girl coming of age in a Chicago Latino neighborhood.

entries.note: Nonfiction writer of Pilgrim at Tinker Creek, a Pulitzer-winning meditation on nature observed near her Virginia home.
Master of extremely short flash fiction, including "Samuel Johnson Is Indignant"; also translated Proust's Swann's Way and Flaubert's Madame Bovary.
Vietnamese American poet and novelist of On Earth We're Briefly Gorgeous, an epistolary novel addressed to the narrator's illiterate mother.

works.note: Ocean Vuong's 2016 debut collection responding to Rilke ("Suppose you do change your life"); includes "Telemachus" and "Aubade with Burning City."

entries.note: Postmodern novelist of The New York Trilogy and 4 3 2 1, blending detective-story tropes with metafictional identity games.

works.note: Three linked detective novellas — City of Glass, Ghosts, and The Locked Room — that dissolve identity and authorship.

entries.note: Vietnam War novelist of The Things They Carried, blurring memoir and fiction; also wrote Going After Cacciato.

works.note: Linked stories cataloging soldiers' physical and emotional burdens; Lieutenant Jimmy Cross blames himself for Kiowa's death in a sewage field.

entries.note: Canadian classicist and poet of the verse novel Autobiography of Red, following the winged monster Geryon; also wrote the brother-elegy Nox.
Mark Z. Danielewski's typographically experimental horror novel about the Navidson family's house, which is larger inside than out.
Novelist of A Thousand Acres, a Pulitzer-winning King Lear retelling set on an Iowa farm.
Dominican American novelist of The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao, following a nerdy Dominican American and his family's curse, or fuku.
Punk postmodernist who used cut-up technique in Blood and Guts in High School and rewrote Don Quixote as an American woman.
Chinese American memoirist of The Woman Warrior, blending myth and autobiography around the legend of Fa Mu Lan.

works.topic: The Woman Warrior: Memoirs of a Girlhood Among Ghosts
works.answerline: The Woman Warrior: Memoirs of a Girlhood Among Ghosts
works.note: Memoir blending the narrator's Chinese American girlhood with the legend of the woman warrior Fa Mu Lan and the fearsome aunt "No Name Woman."

entries.note: Rudolfo Anaya's Chicano coming-of-age novel: Antonio Marez is mentored by the curandera Ultima and learns of the sacred Golden Carp.
Story writer of Jesus' Son and the National Book Award-winning Vietnam epic Tree of Smoke.

works.note: Linked stories narrated by an unnamed addict nicknamed "Fuckhead," including "Emergency" with the orderly Georgie.

entries.note: Yaa Gyasi's debut novel tracing two half-sisters, Effia and Esi, and their descendants from the Gold Coast slave trade to America.
Short story writer known for wordplay and dark comedy, including the collection Birds of America.
Poet-novelist of "Rape Joke" and the memoir Priestdaddy, about growing up with a married Catholic priest father; also wrote the internet-age novel No One Is Talking About This.

entries.topic: A Heartbreaking Work of Staggering Genius
entries.answerline: A Heartbreaking Work of Staggering Genius
entries.note: Dave Eggers's metafictional memoir about raising his younger brother Toph after their parents' deaths.
Graphic memoirist of Fun Home, about her closeted gay father and a family funeral home; the "Bechdel test" bears her name.
Graphic novelist of Maus, depicting the Holocaust with Jews as mice and Nazis as cats through his father Vladek's story.
Poet-novelist of Leaving the Atocha Station and 10:04; The Topeka School follows his autofictional alter ego Adam Gordon.
Novelist of The Secret History, about classics students who murder a classmate, and the Pulitzer-winning The Goldfinch.
Novelist of Station Eleven, following a Shakespearean traveling troupe after a flu pandemic collapses civilization.
Blackfeet novelist of Winter in the Blood, set on the Fort Belknap Reservation, and the historical epic Fools Crow.
Novelist of The Virgin Suicides and the Pulitzer-winning Middlesex.

works.note: Intersex narrator Cal/Calliope Stephanides traces a recessive gene through three generations of a Greek American family in Detroit.

entries.note: Brooklyn novelist of Motherless Brooklyn, narrated by the Tourettic detective Lionel Essrog, and The Fortress of Solitude.
Novelist of Gilead, an epistolary novel from an aging Congregationalist minister to his young son.

works.note: Iowa minister John Ames writes a long letter to his young son, reflecting on his friendship with Boughton and his prodigal namesake.

entries.note: Novelist of The Hours, interweaving Virginia Woolf's writing of Mrs. Dalloway with two later women's lives.
Short story writer of For the Relief of Unbearable Urges and "What We Talk About When We Talk About Anne Frank."
Novelist of My Year of Rest and Relaxation, about a woman who sleeps for a year, and the unsettling Eileen.
Novelist of The Shipping News, set in Newfoundland, and the short story "Brokeback Mountain."
Novelist of The Overstory, a Pulitzer-winning novel of interconnected lives entwined with trees.
Karen Russell's novel of the Bigtree family's failing alligator-wrestling theme park in the Florida Everglades.
Nigerian American novelist of Open City, following psychiatric resident Julius wandering Manhattan; also coined the phrase "white savior industrial complex."
Nicholson Baker's debut novel of office worker Howie's digressive thoughts while riding an escalator during his lunch break.
Memoirist of This Boy's Life, chronicling his turbulent Pacific Northwest childhood under an abusive stepfather.
Cheyenne/Arapaho novelist of There There, following twelve Native characters converging on a powwow in Oakland.
Maggie Nelson's book of 240 numbered propositions on the color blue, named for a Joan Mitchell painting and recounting a breakup and a friend's paralysis.

sections.name: Genre Fiction: Science Fiction, Horror, and Crime
sections.blurb: American genre writing from Golden Age science fiction through mid-century gothic horror to the hardboiled detective novel.

entries.note: Science-fiction lyricist: Fahrenheit 451 with fireman Guy Montag, The Martian Chronicles, Something Wicked This Way Comes, and Dandelion Wine.

works.note: Dystopia of fireman Guy Montag, who burns books at the title temperature, guided by Professor Faber against a society that outlaws reading.
Dark fantasy of boys Will Halloway and Jim Nightshade menaced by Mr. Dark's sinister carnival, Cooger & Dark's Pandemonium Shadow Show.
Story of a nursery that renders children's African-veldt fantasies real, ending with the parents devoured by illusory lions.
Story of an automated house that keeps its routines after a nuclear war kills its inhabitants, leaving only their silhouettes seared onto a wall.
Linked stories of human colonization of Mars, from telepathic Martians' near-extinction to settlers rebuilding Earth culture on the red planet.

entries.note: Prolific SF grandmaster: the Foundation series built on Hari Seldon's psychohistory, the Three Laws of Robotics from I, Robot, and the story "Nightfall."
Master of anthropological SF and fantasy: The Left Hand of Darkness, The Dispossessed, the Earthsea books, and "The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas."

works.note: Utopian city whose happiness depends on a suffering scapegoat child locked in a closet, in "The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas."

entries.note: Chinese American SF author of "Story of Your Life," basis for the film Arrival, in which linguist Louise Banks decodes the alien language Heptapod B; also "Tower of Babylon" and Exhalation.
Cyberpunk pioneer who coined "cyberspace" in "Burning Chrome"; Neuromancer follows hacker Case and the augmented "razorgirl" Molly Millions through the Sprawl.
New Wave SF author of "I Have No Mouth, and I Must Scream," in which the sentient supercomputer AM tortures the last five humans alive; edited the anthology Dangerous Visions.
Walter M. Miller Jr.'s post-nuclear novel of a monastic order preserving human knowledge across centuries, told in sections "Fiat Homo," "Fiat Lux," "Fiat Voluntas Tua."
Gothic writer of "The Lottery," which drew furious New Yorker mail over Tessie Hutchinson's stoning, The Haunting of Hill House, and We Have Always Lived in the Castle.

works.note: Village ritual in which townspeople draw slips of paper, ending with the "winner" Tessie Hutchinson stoned by her neighbors and children.
Novel of parapsychologist John Montague's supernatural investigation at Hill House, where the fragile Eleanor Vance is drawn toward her death.
Alias for the author herself, subject of Ruth Franklin's biography, Susan Scarf Merrell's novel of a couple moving into her house, and a Josephine Decker biopic.

works.topic: We Have Always Lived in the Castle
works.answerline: We Have Always Lived in the Castle
works.note: Novel narrated by Merricat Blackwood, whose family was poisoned with arsenic-laced sugar; she and sister Constance live isolated afterward.
The "not sane" mansion itself, investigated by parapsychologist John Montague and cared for by the Dudleys; setting of, and distinct from, the novel titled for it.

entries.note: Cosmic horror author of the Cthulhu Mythos; "The Call of Cthulhu," At the Mountains of Madness, and the Necronomicon's "Great Old Ones."
Prolific horror author of Carrie, The Shining, and It, set largely in fictional Maine towns like Castle Rock and Derry; also wrote the memoir On Writing.
Hardboiled crime novelist who defined the noir detective in his essay "The Simple Art of Murder"; The Big Sleep and The Long Goodbye.

works.note: Chandler's Los Angeles private eye, hired by General Sternwood in The Big Sleep and searching for the vanished Terry Lennox in The Long Goodbye.

entries.note: Dashiell Hammett's novel of detective Sam Spade pursuing a valuable statuette after his partner Miles Archer's murder; femme fatale Brigid O'Shaughnessy.
African American author of Harlem detective novels starring Coffin Ed Johnson and Gravedigger Jones; also wrote If He Hollers Let Him Go.
Richard Connell's story of hunter Rainsford, shipwrecked on Ship-Trap Island and hunted for sport by General Zaroff.
Common-link genre spanning Owen Wister's The Virginian, Zane Grey's Riders of the Purple Sage, Charles Portis's True Grit, and Larry McMurtry's Lonesome Dove.

sections.name: Places in American Literature
sections.blurb: Cities, states, regions, and countries that recur as common-link answers, tying together works set there or written about them.

entries.note: Ubiquitous common link: Whitman's ferries, Hart Crane's bridge, the Harlem Renaissance, Bartleby's Wall Street, and Gatsby's West Egg parties all feed clues.
Common link: Whitman's "Crossing Brooklyn Ferry," Hart Crane's The Bridge (named for the Brooklyn Bridge), Betty Smith's A Tree Grows in Brooklyn, and Lethem's Fortress of Solitude.
Usually Hughes's poem asking "What happens to a dream deferred?" — "does it dry up / like a raisin in the sun?" — from Montage of a Dream Deferred; sometimes the neighborhood as a common link.
Common link: Sandburg's "Hog Butcher for the World" poem, Sister Carrie, Native Son, The Adventures of Augie March, A Raisin in the Sun, and Upton Sinclair's The Jungle.
Home of the "Boston Brahmins" of Holmes's Elsie Venner, James's The Bostonians (Olive Chancellor and Verena Tarrant), Bellamy's Looking Backward, and Lowell's "For the Union Dead."
Common link: the Beats at City Lights and the Six Gallery "Howl" reading, Amy Tan's Chinatown, Hammett's The Maltese Falcon, and Kerouac's Big Sur trips.
Common link: Didion's Play It As It Lays and The White Album, Nathanael West's The Day of the Locust, Chandler's Philip Marlowe novels, and Pynchon's Inherent Vice.
Common link: Toole's A Confederacy of Dunces, Percy's The Moviegoer, and Tennessee Williams's Vieux Carré, Orpheus Descending, and A Streetcar Named Desire.
Common link via Steinbeck's valleys, Didion's essays, Bret Harte's mining camps, the Los Angeles of Chandler and Nathanael West, and "A Supermarket in California."
Common link: Puritan Boston in The Scarlet Letter, Dickinson's Amherst, Thoreau's Walden Pond, Lowell's "For the Union Dead," and the Salem of The Crucible.
Common link covering both state and river: Faulkner's Yoknapatawpha, home state of Welty and Wright, and Twain's Life on the Mississippi and Huck's raft.
Common link: Hurston's Eatonville in Their Eyes Were Watching God, Stevens's "The Idea of Order at Key West," Nilo Cruz's Anna in the Tropics, and Hiaasen's crime novels.
Common link: Mitchell's Gone with the Wind, O'Connor's home state, Toomer's Cane, and McCullers's The Heart Is a Lonely Hunter.
Common link: Sarah Orne Jewett's Dunnet Landing in The Country of the Pointed Firs, Strout's Olive Kitteridge, and Millay's Mount Battie in "Renascence."
Common link: Robinson's Gilead and Smiley's A Thousand Acres, both set here, plus the Iowa Writers' Workshop that trained Carver and Jorie Graham.
Common link on the country itself: Whitman's "I Hear America Singing," Hughes's "I, Too, Sing America," Ginsberg's "America," McKay's sonnet "America," and Dos Passos's U.S.A. trilogy.
Common link as a destination of escape: fugitive slaves head there in Uncle Tom's Cabin and Beloved, and it recurs as a border-crossing setting and refuge across American fiction.
Common link: Tennessee Williams's The Night of the Iguana, McCarthy's All the Pretty Horses, Chandler's The Long Goodbye, and the crossover poets Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz and Octavio Paz.
Expatriate common link: Hemingway's A Moveable Feast, Stein's salon at 27 rue de Fleurus, Baldwin's Giovanni's Room, and Henry Miller's Tropic of Cancer.
Common link as the expatriate destination behind the Paris entries above: the country Hemingway, Stein, Baldwin, and Miller wrote from or about.
Common link led by Pearl S. Buck's The Good Earth and her Nobel Prize, plus Amy Tan's mother-daughter novels and Maxine Hong Kingston's The Woman Warrior.
Common link: Wheatley's "On Being Brought from Africa to America," Cullen's "Heritage" ("What is Africa to me?"), and the Caribbean poet Derek Walcott's "A Far Cry from Africa."
Setting of Kate Chopin's The Awakening and "Désirée's Baby," and of Ignatius Reilly's New Orleans in A Confederacy of Dunces.
Twain's Life on the Mississippi and the raft of Huckleberry Finn; also Faulkner's "Old Man" and Eliot's "The Dry Salvages."
Hawthorne's "Young Goodman Brown" and The House of the Seven Gables; site of the 1692 witch trials dramatized in Miller's The Crucible.
Wallace Stevens's "The Idea of Order at Key West" and Elizabeth Bishop's island poems; longtime home of Hemingway.
Setting of Erdrich's Love Medicine, Silko's Ceremony, and Alexie's The Lone Ranger and Tonto Fistfight in Heaven and The Absolutely True Diary of a Part-Time Indian.
Setting of Uncle Tom's Cabin and Chesnutt's Conjure Woman tales; also the lost estates of The Little Foxes and A Streetcar Named Desire.
Sherwood Anderson's fictional Winesburg; also home state in Toni Morrison's Sula and The Bluest Eye and David Foster Wallace's The Broom of the System.
Sandburg's "Chicago" and Masters's Spoon River Anthology; Chicago setting of A Raisin in the Sun and Shepard's Buried Child.
Dust Bowl home of Steinbeck's Joad family; also Momaday's Kiowa homeland and the Lynn Riggs play behind the musical Oklahoma!
Franklin's Autobiography and Charles Brockden Brown's Gothic novels; site of the yellow fever epidemic in Arthur Mervyn.
Setting of Hellman's Watch on the Rhine, Edward P. Jones's Lost in the City, and Saunders's Lincoln in the Bardo.
Title structure of Hart Crane's The Bridge; also lends its name to Arthur Miller's A View from the Bridge.
Home of Nathanael West's Day of the Locust and Bukowski's Henry Chinaski; setting of Fitzgerald's The Last Tycoon.
Setting of Chabon's alternate-history The Yiddish Policemen's Union and Krakauer's Into the Wild; homesteader poet John Haines's home state.
Setting of One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest, Vonnegut's "2 B R 0 2 B," and hospital scenes in Angels in America.
Setting of Henry James's Daisy Miller and The Portrait of a Lady, Hawthorne's "Rappaccini's Daughter," and Hemingway's postwar stories.
Where Daisy Miller dies and Isabel Archer returns in James; also Wharton's "Roman Fever" and the "Eternal City" chapter of Catch-22.
Title city of Fitzgerald's "Babylon Revisited" and Stephen Vincent Benét's "By the Waters of Babylon."
Setting of Bradbury's The Martian Chronicles and Tracy K. Smith's Pulitzer-winning Life on Mars.
Birth country of poet Ocean Vuong and novelist Viet Thanh Nguyen, whose The Sympathizer follows a Vietnamese narrator to Los Angeles.
Common-link Brooklyn amusement district: Ferlinghetti's *A Coney Island of the Mind*, Schwartz's "In Dreams Begin Responsibilities," Heller's memoir *Then and Now*.
Common-link ethnic enclave: Frank Chin's *The Year of the Dragon*, Charles Yu's screenplay-format novel, Polanski's neo-noir film of the same name.
Common-link film-industry setting: West's *The Day of the Locust*, Fitzgerald's unfinished *The Last Tycoon*, Didion's *Play It as It Lays*.
Common-link Native setting: Sherman Alexie's Spokane-reservation stories, Erdrich's North Dakota novels, Momaday's *House Made of Dawn*.
Egyptian city common-linking Cavafy's poems, Mark Doty's AIDS-elegy collection *My Alexandria*, and Durrell's *Alexandria Quartet*.
Common-link setting of H.D.'s *Helen in Egypt* and *Kora in Hell*, and of Naguib Mahfouz's Cairo-set novels.
Common-link gaucho-literature homeland of Güiraldes's *Don Segundo Sombra*, Ernesto Sábato, and Manuel Puig.
Common-link home country of Mishima, Ōe, and Dazai, also invoked in WWII bombing poems by James Dickey and John Hersey's *Hiroshima*.
State setting of Irving's "Rip Van Winkle" and "The Legend of Sleepy Hollow," Cather's "Paul's Case," and Kushner's Angels in America.
Setting of Barth's Chesapeake novels: The Sot-Weed Factor, The Floating Opera, The End of the Road, "Lost in the Funhouse."
Setting of Cormac McCarthy's Blood Meridian, All the Pretty Horses, and No Country for Old Men; also McMurtry's Lonesome Dove.
Setting of Walker Percy's The Moviegoer, Toole's A Confederacy of Dunces, and Algren's A Walk on the Wild Side.
Setting of Hunter S. Thompson's Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas, chronicling Raoul Duke and Dr. Gonzo's drug-fueled trip.
Beat Generation hub (City Lights Bookstore); setting of Didion's "Slouching Towards Bethlehem" and Maupin's Tales of the City.
Gertrude Stein's hometown ("there is no there there"), the title source for Tommy Orange's novel There There.
Homeland of Nuyorican and island-born playwrights: Piñero's Short Eyes, Hudes's Water by the Spoonful, and Lin-Manuel Miranda's heritage.
Homeland of poets Robert Burns, Hugh MacDiarmid, and Edwin Muir, and novelists James Kelman and Irvine Welsh (Trainspotting).
Premiere venue for O'Neill's Long Day's Journey Into Night; homeland of Selma Lagerlöf and Scandinavian figures in Crane's "The Blue Hotel."
Setting of Uris's Mila 18 and part of Wiesel's Night; homeland of poets Adam Mickiewicz and Zbigniew Herbert.

sections.name: History and Culture through Literature
sections.blurb: Wars, historical figures, and the institutions — magazines, prizes, critics — that American literature keeps returning to.

entries.note: Common link anchored by Tim O'Brien's The Things They Carried and Going After Cacciato, with veteran poets like Yusef Komunyakaa ("Facing It") close behind.
Common link: The Red Badge of Courage, Whitman's Drum-Taps and hospital service, Benét's John Brown's Body, "An Occurrence at Owl Creek Bridge," and Cold Mountain.
Common link: Dos Passos's U.S.A. trilogy (Nineteen-Nineteen), e.e. cummings's imprisonment recounted in The Enormous Room, and Hemingway's A Farewell to Arms.
Common link: Vonnegut's Slaughterhouse-Five, Randall Jarrell's "The Death of the Ball Turret Gunner," and Auden's "September 1, 1939."
Common link: DeLillo's Falling Man, Jonathan Safran Foer's Extremely Loud and Incredibly Close, Pynchon's Bleeding Edge, and Amiri Baraka's "Somebody Blew Up America."
Common link: Philip Freneau, "poet of the Revolution," Emerson's "Concord Hymn," Longfellow's "Paul Revere's Ride," and the wartime theater bans that Cato defied at Valley Forge.
Common link on revolution as an abstract, ongoing event rather than one war: Gil Scott-Heron's "The Revolution Will Not Be Televised" and a Joy Harjo poem on its "real" form.
Common link: Hart Crane's The Bridge ("Ave Maria"), Joel Barlow's epic The Vision of Columbus, Philip Roth's Goodbye, Columbus, and Wheatley's Columbia-as-goddess.
Common link: Phillis Wheatley's "To His Excellency General Washington" (Columbia's champion) and Parson Weems's myth-making biography.
Common link: William Wells Brown's Clotel (fictionalizing Sally Hemings's daughter), Jefferson's own Notes on the State of Virginia, and his dismissive verdict on Phillis Wheatley's poetry.

Literary common link: Whitman's elegies "O Captain! My Captain!" and "When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd," Sandburg's multivolume biography, Lindsay's "Abraham Lincoln Walks at Midnight," and Saunders's Lincoln in the Bardo.
Common link on the abolitionist in literature: Benét's epic John Brown's Body, Thoreau's "A Plea for Captain John Brown," and James McBride's The Good Lord Bird.
Common link: Benét's "The Devil and Daniel Webster" (defending Jabez Stone), Whittier's poem "Ichabod," and the jumping frog Twain names after him.
Common link as an object of academic study inside fiction: DeLillo's White Noise (Professor Gladney's "Hitler studies") and Running Dog, and W. D. Snodgrass's The Führer Bunker.
Mythological common link: H.D.'s "Helen" and "Helen in Egypt," Poe's "To Helen," Hart Crane's "For the Marriage of Faustus and Helen," and W. C. Williams's "Asphodel, That Greeny Flower."
The magazine as an answerline: it published "The Lottery" to record hate mail, ran Salinger, Cheever, and Updike's stories, devoted an issue to Hersey's "Hiroshima," and employed Parker and Thurber.
Common link: published Raymond Carver's minimalist stories under editor Gordon Lish, ran Fitzgerald's "The Crack-Up" essays, and printed Mailer's and Gay Talese's New Journalism pieces.
Yale literary critic of The Anxiety of Influence and The Western Canon; appears both as a direct answerline and as a book characters read in campus fiction.
Institution/prize common link: the Library of Congress post held by Hayden (first Black laureate), Harjo (first Native American), and recent holders Limón and Sze.
Common link on the ethnicity of Amy Tan, Maxine Hong Kingston, David Henry Hwang, and Frank Chin, whose works explore immigrant identity and generational conflict.
Common-link religious figure: nativity and Passion plays, Adrienne Kennedy's Funnyhouse of a Negro, Lew Wallace's Ben-Hur.
Ancient Greek geometer invoked in Edna St. Vincent Millay's sonnet "Euclid alone has looked on Beauty bare" and a Vachel Lindsay poem about him.
Film icon and third wife of Arthur Miller; recurs as a biographical clue attached to his plays.
Surname of executed Cold War spies Julius and Ethel; their ghosts haunt Roy Cohn in Angels in America, and their execution opens Plath's The Bell Jar.
Backdrop for veteran drama and fiction: Hudes's Water by the Spoonful, Joseph's Bengal Tiger at the Baghdad Zoo, Klay's Redeployment, Powers's The Yellow Birds.
Common link on enslavement: Jacobs-Jenkins's An Octoroon, Harris's Slave Play, August Wilson's Joe Turner's Come and Gone, Butler's Kindred.
Setting or alma mater across fiction and essay: Quentin Compson's college, Emerson's "American Scholar" address, poets Tracy K. Smith and Natasha Trethewey.
Boston abolitionist magazine first edited by James Russell Lowell; published Howe's "Battle Hymn of the Republic" and Coates's "The Case for Reparations."
Common-link industry: Ida B. Wells's and Nellie Bly's journalism, West's Miss Lonelyhearts advice column, Jefferson's "newspapers without government" line.
Influential American poetry critic; her readings of Dickinson, Plath, and other poets recur as biographical or critical clues.
Newspaper of record clued via its crossword, style magazine, and the viral 2021 article "Who Is the Bad Art Friend?"
Literary magazine whose "Writers at Work"/"Art of Fiction" interview series and 2017 editorial scandal are common-link subjects.
Elegized in Whitman's "O Captain! My Captain!" and "When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd"; dramatized in Parks's *Topdog/Underdog* and *Oh, Mary!*.
Executed pirate whose buried-treasure legend inspires Irving's "The Devil and Tom Walker" and Poe's "The Gold-Bug."
Common-link conflict spanning O'Brien's *The Things They Carried*, Denis Johnson's "Emergency," Rabe's war-plays trilogy, and Lauro's *A Piece of My Heart*.
Common-link AIDS-crisis decade of Kushner's *Angels in America*, Kramer's *The Normal Heart*, Wasserstein's *The Heidi Chronicles*, Henley's *Crimes of the Heart*.
Common-link art form in Morrison's *Jazz*, Petry's *The Street*, Walker's *The Color Purple*, and Ellison's essay collection *Living with Music*.
Nobel Prize-winning singer-songwriter; clued via his Beat-influenced lyrics, "Brownsville Girl" co-write with Sam Shepard, and the 2016 Literature Prize controversy.
Singer profiled without an interview in Gay Talese's "Frank Sinatra Has a Cold," a founding text of New Journalism.
American feminist critic who coined "gynocriticism"; author of *A Literature of Their Own* and *The Female Malady*.
Boston magazine that published Rebecca Harding Davis, Charles Chesnutt, and Julia Ward Howe's "Battle Hymn of the Republic."
Ethnic group of authors Sherman Alexie, N. Scott Momaday, and Joy Harjo, and subject of Longfellow's "Song of Hiawatha."
Backdrop for Melville's Battle-Pieces, Howe's "Battle Hymn," Bierce's war stories, and plays on Lincoln's assassination.
Subject of poems by Claude McKay and Countee Cullen and stories by Richard Wright and James Baldwin ("Going to Meet the Man").
1925 Tennessee evolution trial covered by H.L. Mencken, fictionalized in Lawrence and Lee's Inherit the Wind.
Setting of Steinbeck's Of Mice and Men and The Grapes of Wrath, Odets's plays, and Kennedy's Ironweed.
Conflict behind Hemingway's For Whom the Bell Tolls and Orwell's Homage to Catalonia; killed poet Federico García Lorca.
Subject of Wiesel's memoir Night, Spiegelman's Maus, and Foer's Everything Is Illuminated.
Assassinated Soviet revolutionary dramatized in David Ives's All in the Timing ("Variations on the Death of Trotsky").
Subject of DeLillo's Falling Man, Foer's Extremely Loud and Incredibly Close, and Pynchon's Bleeding Edge.
Home of the Iowa Writers' Workshop, long directed by Paul Engle; faculty have included Vonnegut, Carver, and Robinson.

sections.name: Words, Names, and Things
sections.blurb: Bare first names, letters, surnames, and everyday objects, animals, and concepts that quizbowl turns into cross-author common links.
