entries.note: First-name common link spanning many authors and characters named John; answerlines this bare usually come from questions accepting any "John," so treat it as a grab-bag rather than one canonical topic.
First-name common link — Frost, Lowell, Penn Warren, and Hayden among poets alone; like "John," it reflects shared-name common-link questions rather than a single canonical answer.
As a bare answerline this has usually meant Henry James via surname-accepting clues, but since 2024 it may also be Percival Everett's Booker-winning novel James — check the clues.
First-name common link: Philip Roth and the colonial "poet of the Revolution" Philip Freneau are the most frequent referents; check clues to distinguish.
First-name common link, almost always the playwright Eugene O'Neill (Long Day's Journey into Night, Mourning Becomes Electra) rather than any other Eugene.
Surname shared by the Imagist poet Amy, the Confessional poet Robert ("For the Union Dead," "Skunk Hour"), and their ancestor James Russell Lowell.
Surname common link: rival "first American novel" claimants William Hill Brown and Charles Brockden Brown, plus Sterling Brown, Jericho Brown, and William Wells Brown's Clotel.
Surname common link: Richard Wright (Native Son, Black Boy) alongside the unrelated poets James and Charles Wright.
Surname common link: the poets Hart Crane (The Bridge, "Chaplinesque") and Stephen Crane (The Red Badge of Courage, "In the Desert").
Shared-initial common link: e. e. cummings's lowercase signature, E. L. Doctorow (Ragtime, City of God), and E. B. White (Charlotte's Web, The Elements of Style).
Adjective common link: Roth's American Pastoral, Mencken's The American Language, Hayden's collection American Journal, and Rankine's "American Lyric" subtitles.
Name shared by two unrelated entities: the small-town title heroine of Booth Tarkington's Pulitzer-winning novel Alice Adams, and the short-story author Alice Adams (Superior Women).
Personified common link: Dickinson's kindly stopping carriage driver, Whitman's dark mother in "Lilacs," and Fences' Troy Maxson wrestling Death, plus countless title clues.
Genre common link: Franklin's Autobiography, The Autobiography of Malcolm X, Stein's The Autobiography of Alice B. Toklas, and Johnson's The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man.
Common link: Malamud's The Natural, Thayer's "Casey at the Bat," the Polo Grounds prologue of DeLillo's Underworld, Roth's The Great American Novel, and Kinsella's Shoeless Joe.
Common link: Hemingway's "The Killers" and The Sun Also Rises, Odets's Golden Boy, Lipsyte's The Contender, and the boxing writing of Liebling and Joyce Carol Oates.
Common link: O'Neill's The Iceman Cometh, Fitzgerald's Tender Is the Night, Jack London's John Barleycorn, Charles Jackson's The Lost Weekend, and Carver's recovery stories.
Common link: the "End of the Line Cafe" of O'Neill's The Iceman Cometh, Auden's "one of the dives / On Fifty-Second Street," and Lynn Nottage's Sweat.
Common link: Hellman's The Children's Hour, Irving's Ichabod Crane, Ernest Gaines's Grant Wiggins, and Strout's Olive Kitteridge.
Common link: Dos Passos's Newsreel sections, the returning Native veterans of Silko's Ceremony and Momaday's House Made of Dawn, and O'Brien's The Things They Carried.
Common link: Plath's posthumous Letters Home, Barth's epistolary novel LETTERS, Poe's "The Purloined Letter," and Robinson's epistolary Gilead.
Common link: Poe's raven, Dickinson's "Hope is the thing with feathers," Jewett's white heron, the caged bird of Dunbar and Angelou, and Whitman's mockingbird.
Common link: Dickinson's tipsy bees, Plath's late "Bee" sequence in Ariel, Whittier's "Telling the Bees," and Sue Monk Kidd's The Secret Life of Bees.
Common link: Robinson Jeffers's "Hurt Hawks" and his inhumanist creed ("I'd sooner... kill a man than a hawk"), and a companion James Wright poem.
Common link: Pynchon's recurring talking dogs — Vineland's epigraph, Mason & Dixon's "Learned English Dog," and Against the Day's Pugnax.
Common link: Frost's "Birches," Kilmer's "Trees," Betty Smith's A Tree Grows in Brooklyn, and the chokecherry tree of Morrison's Beloved.
Object common link: William Carlos Williams's "This Is Just to Say," the icebox note that became an internet meme, and its companion poem "To a Poor Old Woman."
Common link: Frost's "Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening," Whittier's "Snow-Bound," Longfellow's "The Cross of Snow," and Hemingway's "The Snows of Kilimanjaro."
Common link: Bradbury's "There Will Come Soft Rains" and "The Long Rain," Hemingway's A Farewell to Arms, and the thunder's rain in Eliot's The Waste Land.
Common link: Baldwin's The Fire Next Time, the one-issue Harlem Renaissance magazine Fire!!, and the purifying fire of Eliot's "Little Gidding."
Common link: Tennessee Williams's Suddenly Last Summer and Summer and Smoke, Wharton's Summer, and Bradbury's Dandelion Wine.
Common link: Hughes's "Harlem" ("What happens to a dream deferred?") and his Montage of a Dream Deferred, and Berryman's Dream Songs.
Common link: Dickinson's "'Hope' is the thing with feathers," and O'Neill's Harry Hope, who runs the bar in The Iceman Cometh.
Common link: Carver's "What We Talk About When We Talk About Love," Auden's "O Tell Me the Truth About Love," and Erdrich's Love Medicine.
Common link: Morrison's Beloved, Henry James's The Turn of the Screw, and Roth's Exit Ghost.
Condition common link: O'Connor's Wise Blood, Carver's "Cathedral," Beckett's Waiting for Godot, and Saramago's novel of the same name.
Common link: Whitman's "I Hear America Singing" and "Song of Myself," Hughes's "I, Too, Sing America," and Dunbar's "Sympathy" (the caged bird "sings").
Common link: Didion's Blue Nights, Maggie Nelson's Bluets, Morrison's The Bluest Eye, and the fading blue eyes of the Doctor T. J. Eckleburg billboard in Gatsby.
Common link: the billboard eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg in The Great Gatsby and the title organ of Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God.
Language common link: the Second Avenue Yiddish theater tradition, Isaac Bashevis Singer's Nobel-winning stories, and plays like Indecent and The Tenth Man that draw on it.
Language common link: spoken by the aristocrats of War and Peace, the language of Camus and other Nobel laureates, and a recurring diacritic in Wallace Stevens's diction.
Common-link first name: George in Albee's Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf, husband of Martha, who taunts guests Nick and Honey with an invented dead son.
Common-link first name: George's wife in Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf; also war correspondent Martha Gellhorn, Hemingway's third wife.
Common-link first name: John Berryman's put-upon alter ego "Henry," addressed as "Mr. Bones," across The Dream Songs.
Common-link first name: poets Richard Wilbur, Richard Eberhart, and Richard Siken; also novelist Richard Brautigan of Trout Fishing in America.
Common-link first name: the Biblical first man of Whitman's "Children of Adam" section of Leaves of Grass.
Common-link first name: Cather's doomed aesthete of "Paul's Case," Morrison's Paul D in Beloved, poet Paul Laurence Dunbar.
Common-link first name: Harriet Beecher Stowe (Uncle Tom's Cabin), Harriet Jacobs (Incidents in the Life of a Slave Girl), and Harriet Wilson, the first Black novelist published in the US.
Common-link surname: poets James Weldon and Georgia Douglas Johnson, story writers Denis and Adam Johnson.
Common-link motif of naming: DeLillo's novel The Names, Lahiri's The Namesake, Adam naming the animals in Genesis.
Common-link addressee/figure: Celie's letters in The Color Purple, Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God, O'Neill's The Great God Brown, Sexton's The Awful Rowing Toward God.
Common-link day: Wallace Stevens's "Sunday Morning" and Robert Hayden's "Those Winter Sundays," both meditating on mortality and a father's labor.
Japanese three-line form taken up by Richard Wright (This Other World) and Jack Kerouac, and adapted by Imagists like Ezra Pound.
Fourteen-line form of Claude McKay's "If We Must Die," Countee Cullen's "Yet Do I Marvel," and Millay's Euclid sonnet.
Nineteen-line French form used in Elizabeth Bishop's "One Art," Sylvia Plath's "Mad Girl's Love Song," and Theodore Roethke's "The Waking."
Medieval Biblical cycle drama (Wakefield, N-Town, Chester); invoked as source material and contrasted with miracle plays.
Common-link profession: New York School figures like John Ashbery, Vietnam-era poets Levertov and Merwin, Bolaño's Visceral Realists.
Common-link language: Ngugi's rejection of it in Decolonizing the Mind, Ha Jin's adopted language, Cisneros's Chicana idiom in The House on Mango Street.
Common-link language: translators Pevear and Volokhonsky, Nabokov's switch away from it, Batuman's The Idiot and Saunders's A Swim in a Pond in the Rain.
Ethnicity of playwrights Nilo Cruz (Anna in the Tropics) and Maria Irene Fornes (Fefu and Her Friends), and novelist Oscar Hijuelos.
Chicano ethnicity of authors Sandra Cisneros, Gloria Anzaldúa, Rudolfo Anaya, and Gary Soto.
Movement traced through Gilman's "The Yellow Wallpaper," Friedan's The Feminine Mystique, and Adrienne Rich's poetry.
Common-link procedure in Hemingway's "Hills Like White Elephants," Irving's The Cider House Rules, and Hansberry's A Raisin in the Sun.
Common-link figure: Plath's "Daddy," Hayden's "Those Winter Sundays," Roethke's "My Papa's Waltz," Vuong's poems to his father.
Plot device of Butler's Kindred and Bradbury's "A Sound of Thunder," frequently compared to Wells's The Time Machine.
Common-link fate in Poe's "The Cask of Amontillado," "Berenice," and "The Premature Burial."
Common-link fate: George and Martha's fictional dead son in Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf, Denis Johnson's "Car Crash While Hitchhiking," Myrtle's death in The Great Gatsby.
Common-link fate: Edna Pontellier's suicide in The Awakening, Ophelia comparisons, Faulkner's Quentin Compson.
Medium of Spiegelman's Maus, Bechdel's Fun Home, and Ware's Jimmy Corrigan.
Common-link animal: Emily Dickinson's "I heard a Fly buzz — when I died," and the flying men of Morrison's Song of Solomon.
Common-link animal: Frost's "Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening," Plath's "Ariel," and Joy Harjo's horse poems.
Common-link animal: the same Frost/Plath/Harjo cluster as "horse," plus Peter Shaffer's Equus and the stage adaptation of War Horse.
Common-link animal: John Updike's Rabbit Angstrom tetralogy, Lennie's dream farm in Of Mice and Men, Mary Chase's Harvey, and the Br'er Rabbit trickster of An Octoroon.
Common-link animal: Bradbury's "A Sound of Thunder," Hwang's M. Butterfly and Golden Child, Nabokov's lepidoptery.
Common-link object: Jesmyn Ward's Salvage the Bones and Sing, Unburied, Sing, Hurston's Eatonville tales.
Common-link structure: Hart Crane's "For the Marriage of Faustus and Helen," Arthur Miller's A View from the Bridge, Bierce's Owl Creek Bridge.
Common-link vessel: Melville's Pequod in Moby-Dick, Billy Budd, and Benito Cereno; Oliver Wendell Holmes's "Old Ironsides."
Common-link material: Salinger's Glass family, Emily St. John Mandel's The Glass Hotel, the glass church of Peter Carey's Oscar and Lucinda.
Common-link object: Sophie Treadwell's Machinal and Elmer Rice's The Adding Machine, both expressionist plays named for the title device.
Common-link substance: Vonnegut's world-ending ice-nine in Cat's Cradle, Frost's "Fire and Ice."
Common-link object: Tom Wolfe's The Kandy-Kolored Tangerine-Flake Streamline Baby, Nelson Angstrom's failing dealership in Rabbit at Rest.
Common-link sport: David Foster Wallace's tennis essays and the Enfield Tennis Academy of Infinite Jest.
Common-link sport: John Updike's Rabbit Angstrom, Sherman Alexie's reservation ball, Owen Meany's shot in A Prayer for Owen Meany.
Common-link occupation: Hemingway's Santiago in The Old Man and the Sea, and Achille and Hector in Derek Walcott's Omeros.
Common-link activity: Santiago's marlin in The Old Man and the Sea, Hemingway's Nick Adams story "Big Two-Hearted River."
Mythological figure of Auden's "Musée des Beaux Arts," William Carlos Williams's "Landscape with the Fall of Icarus," and poems by Sexton and Rukeyser.
Bare first name for Susan Glaspell, author of *Trifles* and *Alison's House*, co-founder of the Provincetown Players.
Bare first name clued mainly via William Faulkner (*As I Lay Dying*, *Sanctuary*); also Vachel Lindsay's "General William Booth Enters into Heaven."
Bare first name for Edward Albee, playwright of *Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?*, *The Goat*, and *The Zoo Story*.
Bare first name for Ernest Hemingway, clued via *For Whom the Bell Tolls*, *To Have and Have Not*, and Gertrude Stein's "Lost Generation" quip.
Bare first name for Eudora Welty, author of "Death of a Traveling Salesman," *The Optimist's Daughter*, and *The Eye of the Story*.
Bare first name for Gwendolyn Brooks ("We Real Cool," *Annie Allen*), sometimes conflated with Harlem Renaissance poet Gwendolyn Bennett.
Bare first name for Adrienne Rich, poet of "Diving into the Wreck" who theorized "compulsory heterosexuality."
Bare letter for W. H. Auden, poet of "September 1, 1939" and "Funeral Blues," clued via his obsessive line revisions.
Bare letter for J. D. Salinger ("A Perfect Day for Bananafish," *Nine Stories*); also clues J. M. Coetzee's autobiographical "John."

Common-link relation in Parks's *Topdog/Underdog*, Wilson's *Fences*, Shepard's *True West*, and Heaney's "Mid-Term Break."
Common-link figures titling E. Nesbit's and Gertrude Chandler Warner's series, clued via Plath's "The Night Dances" and "Edge."
Common-link profession of Heller's Daneeka, Lewis's Martin Arrowsmith, Fitzgerald's T. J. Eckleburg, and Nick Adams's father in Hemingway.

Common-link figures in Sexton's "Her Kind," Frost's dialogues, Updike's *The Witches of Eastwick*, and Miller's *The Crucible*.
Common-link locale in Sartre's *No Exit* ("hell is other people"), Rimbaud's *A Season in Hell*, and Williams's *Kora in Hell*.
Religion behind the Transcendentalists' "Boston Brahmin," clued via Emerson's "Hamatreya" and Thoreau's *Walden*.
Common-link language/ethnicity in Plath's "Daddy," Twain's "The Awful German Language," and the Nobel wins of Herta Müller and Günter Grass.
Common-link genre behind Ashbery's translations of D'Aulnoy, Angela Carter's *The Bloody Chamber*, and Glück's "Gretel in Darkness."
Poetic technique in Marianne Moore's "The Fish" and Williams's "The Red Wheelbarrow," discussed by critics Culler and Agamben.
First title-word of Wallace Stevens's "Anecdote of the Jar," from *Harmonium*.
Common-link medium in poems by Eileen Myles, Frank Bidart, and Danez Smith, and Fitzgerald's Hollywood novel *The Last Tycoon*.
Common-link medium in Danielewski's *House of Leaves* and Wallace's *Infinite Jest*, and adaptations like Polanski's *Death and the Maiden*.
Common-link medium of Bechdel's *Fun Home*, Yang's *American Born Chinese*, and Drnaso's *Sabrina*.
Common-link reference-book genre: Samuel Johnson's, Ambrose Bierce's *Devil's Dictionary*, and Flaubert's *Dictionary of Received Ideas*.
Common-link event in Dickinson's "I felt a Funeral, in my Brain," Auden's "Funeral Blues," and Stevens's "The Emperor of Ice-Cream."
Common-link procedure in Hemingway's "Hills Like White Elephants," Irving's *The Cider House Rules*, and poems by Brooks and Sexton.
Common-link body art in O'Connor's "Parker's Back," Bradbury's *The Illustrated Man*, and Karen Russell's "The New Veterans."
Common-link setting of Cheever's "The Swimmer," Salinger's "Teddy," and Wallace's "Forever Overhead."
Common-link structures in Cisneros's *The House on Mango Street*, Machado's *In the Dream House*, and Woolf's "A Haunted House."
Biblical figure reimagined as a banker in MacLeish's *J.B.* and as Mendel Singer's namesake in Joseph Roth's novel *Job*.
Common-link object titling Ishiguro's *Klara and the Sun* and Ballard's *Empire of the Sun*, and clued in Shakespeare's Sonnet 130.
Common-link object that bludgeons Roberta in Dreiser's *An American Tragedy*; also wielded in a Cortázar story.
Common-link fruit in Frost's "After Apple-Picking," Yeats's "The Song of Wandering Aengus," and Neruda's "Ode to the Apple."
Common-link fruit tied to Bashō's and Yoshimoto's pen names, and to Asturias's banana-trilogy novels.
Common-link flower in Faulkner's *The Sound and the Fury* and Wordsworth's "I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud."
Common-link foodstuff in Ogden Nash's light verse and Steinbeck's *Of Mice and Men* ("livin' off the fatta' the lan'").
Common-link objects in Bradbury's *Fahrenheit 451*, Hawthorne's "The Custom-House," and Anne Bradstreet's "The Author to Her Book."
Common-link objects in Poe's "The Bells," Dickinson's "I felt a Funeral, in my Brain," and Ransom's "Bells for John Whiteside's Daughter."
Common-link drinking establishment: O'Neill's *The Iceman Cometh*, Nottage's *Sweat* and *Ruined*, Gordone's *No Place to Be Somebody*.
Common-link setting of Hemingway's "A Clean, Well-Lighted Place" and "The Killers," and David Ives's *Sure Thing*.
Common-link setting of Whitman's "Out of the Cradle Endlessly Rocking" and "As I Ebb'd with the Ocean of Life."
Common-link animal in London's *The Call of the Wild*, Steinbeck's *Of Mice and Men*, Albee's *The Zoo Story*, Woolf's *Flush*.
Common-link animal in Faulkner's "The Bear" and Steinbeck's *Of Mice and Men* (Lennie moves "like a creeping bear").
Common-link animal in Twain's "The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County" and Joel Chandler Harris's Uncle Remus tales.

Bare first name shared by poets Louise Bogan and Louise Glück (Nobel laureate, "Mock Orange," The Wild Iris).
Bare first name for novelist Mary McCarthy (The Group), story writer Mary Gaitskill (Bad Behavior), and O'Neill's Mary Tyrone.
Bare first name for New Journalism author Norman Mailer, of The Naked and the Dead and The Executioner's Song.
Bare first name shared by Faulkner's doomed Quentin Compson (The Sound and the Fury) and the narrator of Miller's After the Fall.
Bare first name for minimalist story writer Raymond Carver (Cathedral, What We Talk About When We Talk About Love).
Bare first name for Thomas Dixon (The Clansman) and reclusive novelist Thomas Pynchon (Gravity's Rainbow, The Crying of Lot 49).
Surname spanning novelist Cormac McCarthy (Blood Meridian), Mary McCarthy (The Group), and Senator Joseph McCarthy's HUAC hearings.
Surname shared by Armenian-American playwright William Saroyan (The Time of Your Life) and his poet son Aram.
Surname spanning poets Tracy K. Smith (Life on Mars), Danez Smith, Patricia Smith, and novelist Zadie Smith (On Beauty).
Surname spanning poet William Carlos Williams, playwright Tennessee Williams, and novelists John A. Williams and John Williams (Stoner).
Surname spanning novelists Thomas Wolfe (Look Homeward, Angel), Tom Wolfe (The Right Stuff), and Tobias Wolff.
Addressee of Ocean Vuong's poetry, Emma Lazarus's "Mother of Exiles," and title figure of Marsha Norman's 'night, Mother.

Profession of Thea Kronborg in Cather's The Song of the Lark and Roxanne Coss in Patchett's Bel Canto.

Profession of Willy Loman (Death of a Salesman) and R.J. Bowman in Eudora Welty's stories.
Profession of the narrators in Chang-rae Lee's Native Speaker and Viet Thanh Nguyen's The Sympathizer.
Activity in Malamud's The Natural (Roy Hobbs), Irving's A Prayer for Owen Meany, and Chaim Potok's The Chosen.

Activity in Cheever's "The Swimmer" (Neddy Merrill), Yates's Revolutionary Road, and Knowles's A Separate Peace.
Color in John Logan's play Red (about Mark Rothko), Crane's The Red Badge of Courage, and Williams's "The Red Wheelbarrow."
Color in Charlotte Perkins Gilman's "The Yellow Wallpaper" and Stephen Crane's "The Bride Comes to Yellow Sky."
Flower in Hawthorne's "Dr. Heidegger's Experiment," Stein's "Rose is a rose is a rose," and Williams's The Rose Tattoo.
Recurring image in Stein's "Sacred Emily," Ocean Vuong's mother's chosen name Rose, and Tupac's "The Rose That Grew from Concrete."
Prop in Eugene O'Neill's The Great God Brown and Mourning Becomes Electra, dramatizing divided consciousness.

Time-of-day motif in poems by Mark Strand and Li Bai; also titles Elie Wiesel's Holocaust memoir Night.
Motif in Whitman's "When I Heard the Learn'd Astronomer" and "When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd"; closing word of each Divine Comedy cantica.

Animal in Brautigan's In Watermelon Sugar, Adrienne Rich's "Aunt Jennifer's Tigers," and Blake's "The Tyger."

Word titling Toni Morrison's Paradise, Fitzgerald's This Side of Paradise, and Yanagihara's To Paradise.

sections.name: Foreign Voices in American Literature Questions
sections.blurb: Non-American authors and works that surface inside American Literature questions, usually as an influence, a translation, or an explicit crossover clue.

entries.note: Czech-German modernist novelist of The Metamorphosis and The Trial; appears via his influence on American postmodern fiction and Borges's essay on his "precursors."
Argentine writer of Ficciones and "The Garden of Forking Paths"; crosses into American Literature questions through his influence on Barth, Barthelme, and other postmodernists.
Spanish poet of Gypsy Ballads, invoked in Ginsberg's "A Supermarket in California" ("what were you doing down by the watermelons?") and Jack Spicer's collection After Lorca.
English poet of "This Be the Verse" and "Aubade"; appears as a crossover answer alongside American poets in mixed 20th-century poetry bonuses.
French Nobel laureate of Penguin Island and The Revolt of the Angels; appears as a crossover foreign-author answer in literature bonuses.
Dutch-German diarist of The Diary of a Young Girl; referenced by American writers including Vonnegut (the epigraph to Galápagos), Nathan Englander, and the Goodrich-Hackett Broadway adaptation.
Postwar novel by German author Heinrich Böll about the Faehmel family; the sampled clues show no explicit American-literature tie — flagging as possibly misfiled in this subcategory.
Scottish novelist of Ivanhoe and Waverley; his historical romances are a recurring point of comparison in American fiction.
English Romantic poet of Childe Harold's Pilgrimage and "She Walks in Beauty"; appears as a character in Tennessee Williams's Camino Real.
English mathematician-author of Alice's Adventures in Wonderland; his "Hunting of the Snark" inspired the title of Jack London's The Cruise of the Snark.
Daniel Defoe's castaway, subject of Elizabeth Bishop's poem "Crusoe in England" and J. M. Coetzee's Foe.
Anthony Burgess's dystopian novel of teen "droog" Alex and the invented slang Nadsat; Kubrick's film adaptation is frequently clued alongside it.
James Joyce's final dream-narrative novel of HCE and Anna Livia Plurabelle; often compared to Thornton Wilder's The Skin of Our Teeth.
Norwegian father of modern realist drama (A Doll's House, Hedda Gabler); recurs as a dramaturgical touchstone.
Ancient Greek fabulist, traditionally depicted as a freed slave; author of "The Tortoise and the Hare" and "The Fox and the Grapes."
Russian novelist of War and Peace and Anna Karenina; the latter is read aloud within Nilo Cruz's Cuban-American play Anna in the Tropics.
Polish-born, Yiddish-language Nobel laureate naturalized in the US; wrote "Gimpel the Fool" and The Magician of Lublin.
Home country of authors Imre Kertész, László Krasznahorkai, Péter Esterházy, and Sándor Márai, clued in translated-literature bonuses.
Czech-French novelist of The Unbearable Lightness of Being; recurs as a philosophical touchstone in discussions of fiction.
French novelist of Submission and The Elementary Particles, provocateur on religion, sex, and cloning.
Argentine novelist obsessed with classic Hollywood film; wrote Kiss of the Spider Woman and Betrayed by Rita Hayworth.
Derek Walcott's epic poem reimagining the Iliad among Caribbean fishermen Achille and Hector; Walcott is a Saint Lucian Nobel laureate.
Japanese novelist known for surreal, genre-blending fiction; recurs as a comparison figure for American postmodernists.
David Mitchell's British novel of six nested narratives spanning 1850 to a post-apocalyptic future, including the replicant Sonmi-451.
J. M. Coetzee's Booker-winning novel of disgraced professor David Lurie, who moves in with his daughter Lucy after an affair with a student.
Roman poet of the *Odes* and *Ars Poetica*, source of "carpe diem," clued via Andrew Marvell's Horatian ode and MacLeish's title borrowing.
English poet of *Paradise Lost*, invoked via Stanley Fish's reader-response criticism of "how Milton works."
English poet of *The Rape of the Lock*, clued via his feud with fellow poet Anne Finch.
English novelist of *Tom Jones* and *Shamela*, a parody of Samuel Richardson.
Victorian art critic of *Modern Painters* and *The Seven Lamps of Architecture*, coiner of "pathetic fallacy," idolized by Proust.
English playwright referenced via Eliot's essay "Hamlet and His Problems" and as the title-nickname joke in *The Glass Menagerie*.

works.note: Shakespeare tragedy comically retyped by three monkeys in David Ives's *Words, Words, Words*.

entries.note: Irish poet of "Easter, 1916" and "The Second Coming"; also clued via "The Song of Wandering Aengus."
British modernist clued via short stories "Kew Gardens," "The Legacy," and "A Mark on the Wall."
Katherine Mansfield story of Bertha Young's dinner party, whose title feeling curdles on discovering husband Harry's affair with Pearl Fulton.
English author of *Sons and Lovers*, *Women in Love*, and stories like "Odour of Chrysanthemums."
English author of *Nineteen Eighty-Four*, *Homage to Catalonia*, and "Shooting an Elephant."
British author of stories involving Old Misery's demolished house and a turtle-soup matricide; wrote the introduction to Highsmith's story collection.
English novelist of the "Campus Trilogy" and critic whose *The Art of Fiction* discusses Salinger's prose style.
Peter Shaffer play in which psychiatrist Martin Dysart treats Alan Strang's obsession with horses.
Irish author of *Brooklyn* (Eilis Lacey's emigration) and *The Master*, a fictionalized life of Henry James.
French author of *Madame Bovary*, clued via Julian Barnes's *Flaubert's Parrot* and his lover Louise Colet.
French playwright of *The Blacks* and novelist whose gender-nonconforming character inspired drag performer Divine's stage name.
Russian poet of "Requiem," mourning her arrested son Lev Gumilev; married to poet Nikolai Gumilev.
Turgenev's hunting-trip story collection ("Khor and Kalinych," "Bezhin Meadow") whose publication led to his house arrest.
Ivan Bunin story of a rich, unnamed American who dies of a heart attack on Capri during a European vacation.
Italian trench poet of "Nostalgia" and "Mattina," founder of the Hermetic school of poetry.
Polish-Jewish author of *The Street of Crocodiles* and *Sanatorium Under the Sign of the Hourglass*, murdered by the Gestapo.
South African anti-apartheid author of *Cry, the Beloved Country* and *Too Late the Phalarope*.
Colombian author of "A Very Old Man with Enormous Wings," *Chronicle of a Death Foretold*, and *Love in the Time of Cholera*.
Argentine author of *Hopscotch* and the story "Axolotl."
Cervantes's novel invoked as a comparison for Bellow's *Henderson the Rain King* and reworked in Borges's and Auster's metafiction.
Lebanese-American author of *The Prophet* (Almustafa's discourses at Orphalese), writing in English after emigrating to the U.S.
Ancient Greek lyric poet of Lesbos ("Ode to Aphrodite"); Anne Carson's translations (If Not, Winter) recur in clues.
Ancient Greek tragedian of Oedipus Rex, Oedipus at Colonus, and Antigone.
Euripides' vengeful sorceress who kills her children; echoed in Cherríe Moraga's The Hungry Woman and Jesmyn Ward's Salvage the Bones.
Arabian Nights storyteller who delays her execution with tales; referenced in Barth's Chimera and Murakami's stories.
Tang Dynasty Chinese poet of "Quiet Night Thoughts" and "Drinking Alone by Moonlight," rendered by Ezra Pound as "The River Merchant's Wife."
Dante's 14th-century epic of Hell, Purgatory, and Paradise; inspired Longfellow's translation and sonnet "Mezzo Cammin."
British legendary king retold by Steinbeck in his unfinished The Acts of King Arthur and His Noble Knights.
Shakespeare's late romance of Prospero, Ariel, and Caliban; adapted postcolonially by Aimé Césaire, echoed in Hayden's "Middle Passage."
18th-century British lexicographer and essayist, subject of Boswell's biography, author of A Dictionary of the English Language.
Scottish novelist of Waverley, Ivanhoe, and The Bride of Lammermoor; Twain blamed him for romanticizing the antebellum South.
Victorian British poet of dramatic monologues ("Porphyria's Lover," "My Last Duchess"); influenced Ezra Pound's Personae.
Arthur Conan Doyle's Sherlock Holmes novel of the phosphorus-doused hound haunting Dartmoor.
Pen name of British writer H.H. Munro, author of "The Open Window," "Sredni Vashtar," and the Reginald stories.
Bengali Nobel laureate poet of Gitanjali; wrote India's and Bangladesh's national anthems, championed by Yeats.
New Zealand-born short story writer of "The Garden Party," "Bliss," and "Miss Brill."
Chinese author of "Diary of a Madman" and The True Story of Ah Q, leading figure of the May Fourth Movement.
French novelist of Journey to the End of the Night, notorious Vichy collaborator; alter ego Bardamu.
Andrei Platonov's Soviet novel of workers digging a foundation for a "House of the Proletariat" amid collectivization.
British poet-memoirist of Good-Bye to All That and I, Claudius; author of the mythography The White Goddess.
Irish playwright of Waiting for Godot, Endgame, and Happy Days; wrote in French and English.
Harold Pinter's play of Teddy bringing wife Ruth to his London family; also the first play of O'Neill's Mourning Becomes Electra.
Dylan Thomas's Welsh radio play of the town Llareggub, narrated by Captain Cat and preacher-poet Eli Jenkins.
Alan Moore's graphic novel deconstructing superheroes (Rorschach, Dr. Manhattan, Ozymandias) amid Cold War paranoia.
British-Indian novelist of Midnight's Children and The Satanic Verses, subject of a fatwa; memoir Joseph Anton.
Arundhati Roy's Booker-winning debut novel of twins Estha and Rahel in Kerala, India.
